title: chore(error-tracking): increase max retries
body:
## Problem

10 retries is a lot - but when cymbal crashes all at once, 3 isn't enough and the ingestion pipeline then crashes too. this gives us breathing room to wait for cymbal to be available again - with the existing backoff logic, this should cap out around 30s.

title: fix(phrocs): use ANSI colors and reduce CPU from ~60% to ~3%
body:
## Problem

phrocs had several usability and performance issues:
- Hardcoded hex colors looked broken on light terminal themes
- CPU usage was ~60% (vs mprocs at 0.6%) due to expensive process tree scanning and excessive render frequency    
- Copy mode included border characters in copied text
- Info view didn't persist when switching processes
- Quit caused a ~10s terminal freeze

## Changes           

**ANSI terminal colors**: replaced hardcoded hex colors with ANSI 4-bit palette

title: fix(dashboards): do not abort tile refresh when saving from edit mode
body:
## Problem

Saving a dashboard from edit mode called `abortAnyRunningQuery()` at the start of `saveEditModeChanges`. That shares the same `AbortController` as an in-flight **preview / tile refresh** (for example after **Apply filters** on a large dashboard, or any full refresh). Saving while tiles were still loading aborted those requests, cleared refresh state badly, and left every tile showing **“Chart data didn’t load”** with no follow-up refresh—an abrupt, broken-looking dashboard.

**How th

title: fix(dashboards): handle numeric search query params from URL
body:
## Problem

On the dashboards list, a search that was entirely digits was written to the URL and triggered a search pass, but the in-memory filter then reset to empty. The input went blank and the table showed unfiltered results. That happened because kea-router can deserialize numeric-looking query values as JavaScript numbers, and the scene only treated `search` as valid when `typeof === 'string'`.

## Changes

Add `urlSearchParamToString` in `dashboardsLogic` and use it when hydrating `

title: fix(llma): prevent traces list crash from heavy input/output rendering
body:
## Problem

The LLMA traces list crashes in Chromium when the `LLM_OBSERVABILITY_SHOW_INPUT_OUTPUT` feature flag is enabled. With many traces loaded, the browser hangs because:

1. Full, un-truncated message content is passed to `LLMMessageDisplay` in minimal mode
2. Markdown rendering runs on the full content for every row, causing expensive AST parsing and DOM creation
3. CSS `max-h-50 overflow-y-auto` only clips visually — the entire DOM is still constructed

The generations list doesn't have

title: fix(llma): normalize OpenAI Responses API items with correct roles
body:
## Problem

OpenAI Responses API items (`function_call`, `function_call_output`, `reasoning`, `web_search_call`, etc.) use `type` instead of `role` to identify the message kind. The `normalizeMessage()` function had no handlers for these types, so they fell through to the unsupported message path and were assigned the `defaultRole` (typically `user` for inputs). This caused function calls and function outputs to display as "user" messages in the trace view.

Reported by a user in https://posthog

title: fix(mcp): remove uv dependency from Cloudflare Pages build
body:
## Problem

The MCP service Cloudflare Pages build fails because `generate-ui-apps.ts` calls `bin/hogli format:js` to format generated files. `hogli` uses `uv run --script`, which checks `pyproject.toml`'s `required-version = "~=0.10.2"` — but Cloudflare's build environment has `uv 0.11.1`, causing a version mismatch error.

## Changes

Replace the `bin/hogli format:js` call in `formatOutput()` with direct `pnpm oxlint` + `pnpm exec oxfmt` invocations — the same commands `hogli format:js` runs i

title: fix(hogql): enable analyzer for non-equality JOIN ON conditions
body:
## Problem

ClickHouse rejects non-equality JOIN ON conditions (e.g. `>=`, `<=`, `>`, `<`, `!=`) with `INVALID_JOIN_ON_EXPRESSION` when the analyzer is not enabled. This means HogQL queries like the following fail:

```sql
SELECT ...
FROM exposures AS e
LEFT JOIN conversions AS c
  ON e.person_id = c.person_id
  AND c.conversion_time >= e.exposure_time
```

> Code: 403. DB::Exception: Unsupported JOIN ON conditions. Unexpected 'conversion_time >= exposure_time' (INVALID_JOIN_ON_EXPRESSION)

Clic

title: fix(alerts): drop incomplete current interval in detector-based alerts
body:
## Problem

Detector-based anomaly alerts (zscore, KNN, isolation forest, etc.) have been firing on every single hourly check with near-100% anomaly probability. This affects all alerts using the `check_trends_alert_with_detector` code path.

The root cause: the query fetches data without setting `date_to`, so the result always includes the **current incomplete interval** as the last data point. At :20 past each hour, the current hour has only ~20 minutes of accumulated data. With `diffs_n=1` pr

title: fix(dagster): use correct Hive partition keys in duckling backfill S3 paths
body:
## Summary

Fixes the `ducklake_add_data_files` error: `"invalid partition value for the table configuration"`.

Root cause traced through [DuckLake source code](https://github.com/duckdb/ducklake/blob/main/src/functions/ducklake_add_data_files.cpp):

- The events table is partitioned by `year(timestamp), month(timestamp), day(timestamp)` — **3 partition fields**
- DuckLake resolves partition values by parsing Hive-style `key=value` segments from the file path, then checks that the count matches

On macOS, `node-gyp` bakes absolute paths into the `node-rdkafka` `.node` binary when linking against bundled dylibs. When `node_modules` is CoW-cloned to a different git worktree, the binary breaks because the paths no longer exist.

Separately, the sandbox infrastructure (`bin/sandbox`, `Dockerfile.sandbox`, `docker-compose.sandbox.yml`) adds complexity and maintenance burden without active use. Service hostname defaults (e.g. `db`, `redis7`, `objectstorage`) assumed a Docker-inter

title: fix(flags): use events_recent for sync_feature_flag_last_called query
body:
## Problem

The `sync_feature_flag_last_called` Celery task hits ClickHouse's 5 TiB read limit after ~34 seconds. The task queries the main `events` table (months/years of data, monthly partitions) for `$feature_flag_called` events across all teams. Without a leading `team_id` filter, the primary key `(team_id, toDate(timestamp), event, ...)` can't be used efficiently, resulting in near-full table scans.

## Changes

Switch the query from the `events` table to `events_recent` — a materiali

title: fix(dagster): disable hive partition inference in ducklake_add_data_files
body:
## Summary

Fixes the `InvalidInputError: invalid partition value for the table configuration` error in the duckling/ducklake backfill jobs.

**Root cause:** `ducklake_add_data_files` auto-infers partition columns from directory structure (both Hive-style `key=value` and positional segments). The S3 paths have segments like `team_id/year/month/day/` but the table uses expression-based partitioning (`year(timestamp), month(timestamp), day(timestamp)`), so DuckLake can't map the directory segments

title: fix(ci): use merge-multiple for single-image digest collection
body:
## Problem

The `build-images / collect digests` job in the Rust smoke test CI fails when only a single image is built (e.g., `image-filter: capture`).

`actions/download-artifact@v7` with `merge-multiple: false` creates subdirectories for multiple artifacts (`/tmp/digests/digest-capture/digest.txt`) but extracts directly to the path for a single artifact (`/tmp/digests/digest.txt`) — no subdirectory. The aggregation script iterates over `digest-*` directories, finds none, and errors with "No di

title: feat(workflow): implement pipeline architecture for massive backfill performance improvement
body:
## Problem

The person properties backfill workflow was taking 237 seconds for just 1,000 persons with 224 cohorts (383,000 events). With 95 million persons total, this would take approximately **260 days** to complete - completely impractical for production use.

Key bottlenecks identified:
- Sequential filter evaluation: 224 cohorts evaluated one by one per person
- Synchronous Kafka flushing blocking processing
- No pipeline parallelism: batches processed sequentially by coordinator

title: fix: query coalescing signal_error
body: ## Problem Query coalescing deduplicates concurrent identical queries by having one request (leader) execute while others (followers) wait and receive the same cached result via Redis. Query coalescing follower crash metric increased and error metric went away after extending them to more endpoints in: https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/52511

title: feat(dagster): add team_id to ducklake events table partitioning
body:
## Summary

The shared DuckLake events table is partitioned by `year(timestamp), month(timestamp), day(timestamp)` — missing `team_id`. Since every query filters by `team_id` first, this means DuckLake must scan partition metadata for all teams even when querying a single team's data.

This adds an idempotent `SET PARTITIONED BY` call in `validate_ducklake_schema` (the pre-flight check that runs before every backfill) to ensure the table is partitioned by:

```sql
PARTITIONED BY (team_id, year(t

title: revert(flags): remove sent_at delta metric for client-to-server transit time
body:
Reverts PostHog/posthog#52648

## Problem

The `sent_at` delta metric added in #52648 doesn't work as intended — only a couple of clients send the `sent_at` field, and they send it in different formats. It's not the right field to measure client-to-server transit time.

Reverting while we come up with a better approach.

## Changes

Removes all code added in #52648 across 3 files:
- `rust/feature-flags/src/api/endpoint.rs` — removes `compute_sent_at_delta` function, its call site, histogram emis

title: feat(flags): add X-Request-Start queue time metric
body:
## Problem

We have no visibility into how long requests sit in the proxy queue (NLB → Contour/Envoy → app pod). Without this, queue-induced latency spikes are invisible in our dashboards.

## Changes

- Parse the `X-Request-Start` header (`t=<epoch_seconds>`) set by Contour and compute `queue_time_ms = now - request_start`
- Use integer arithmetic to avoid f64 precision loss when converting seconds to milliseconds
- Reject negative values, non-finite floats (NaN, Infinity), and malformed header

Formula-based trends queries (e.g. bounce rate = A/B*100) with aggregated displays (`ActionsBarValue`) return results where `action` is `null`. title: fix(ws): reduce noise from Kafka commit warnings during rebalance
body:
## Problem

These warnings fire on every Kafka rebalance even when there are simply no offsets to commit

## Changes

title: fix(dagster): don't report dry runs as failures in part breaker
body:
## Problem

Dry runs of the part breaker return `None` from `break_part` (by design as no modifications are made). But `report_results` treats all `None` results as failures, so dry runs raise `dagster.Failure`.

Since each Dagster op has its own config namespace, passing a `dry_run` config flag to `report_results` would require setting it in multiple places.

## Changes

- Add `dry_run: bool` field to `BreakResult` dataclass
- Dry runs now return `BreakResult(part=part, dry_run=True)`

Without this, allowlisted teams that should be exempt from rate limiting on the `/flags/definitions` endpoint get blocked by the Rust service.

title: fix(grouping): provide next available index to backfill deleted group types
body:
## Problem

If you have 5 group types, and delete one of them from your dashboard: you cannot create a new group type even though you only have 4 group types now. 

For example, if you have the following group types:

```
group type name => group type index
A => 0
B => 1
C => 2
D => 3
E => 4
```

If you now delete group type `E` (index 4), and create a new group type `F`, you'll end up with

```
group type name => group type index
A => 0
B => 1
C => 2
D => 3
F => 4
```

title: fix(alerts): use pyod unify normalization for zscore and iqr detectors
body:
## Problem

The zscore and IQR anomaly detectors applied `erf` directly to raw z-scores/distances, producing uniformly distributed scores on [0,1] for normally distributed data. This means `threshold=0.95` always produces a ~5% false positive rate regardless of how stable the data is. When combined in an ensemble with OR logic alongside pyod-backed detectors (which use a different score distribution), the zscore detector dominates and causes excessive alert firings on stable data.

## Changes

-

title: fix(dagster): retry experimental sessions backfill on ClickHouse OOM
body:
## Problem

The experimental sessions v3 backfill can fail with ClickHouse `MEMORY_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` (error code 241) when a single chunk requires too much memory. This crashes the entire dagster run and requires manual intervention.

## Changes

- On OOM, catch the error and retry the failed chunk by splitting it into 10 sub-queries using `cityHash64(distinct_id)` range filtering
- Each sub-query covers 1/10th of the distinct_id hash space, reducing memory pressure
- Limited to one retry per chun

title: fix(ci): fix ghcommit-action glob patterns silently dropping product OpenAPI types
body:
## Root cause

PR #52220 (merged Mar 25) refactored the OpenAPI auto-commit from `git add` + `git push` to `ghcommit-action`. The same glob patterns were copied verbatim, but the two approaches handle globs differently:

- **Old**: `git add products/*/frontend/generated/` — the **shell** expands `*` before passing to git ✅
- **New**: `file_pattern: 'products/*/frontend/generated/'` — passed as a literal string to ghcommit-action, which feeds it to `git status -- <pathspec>`. Git does **not** exp

title: feat(flags): support mixed person+group filters in single condition
body:
## Problem

Customers want flags like "50% of enterprise companies, but only pro-tier users within those companies." This requires a single condition to combine person-level and group-level property filters with group-aggregated rollout (Scenario 3 from the mixed targeting design in #33206).

The Rust evaluation service previously loaded only one property source per condition based on aggregation mode, so person filters in a group-aggregated condition would be checked against group propertie

title: fix: use _offset instead of _timestamp in HogQL realtime cohort query
body:
## Problem

The HogQL realtime cohort query was using `_timestamp` instead of `_offset` in the `argMax` function, which could lead to incorrect temporal ordering when determining the most recent match state for persons in cohorts.

## Changes

- Updated `argMax(matches, _timestamp)` to `argMax(matches, _offset)` in the realtime cohort query
- This ensures correct ordering based on the ClickHouse offset rather than timestamp

PR #47130 introduced a HogQL permission layer that removes `system.*` tables when `user=None` is passed to`Database.create_for(`. Two other paths still create `InsightContext` without passing `user`:

- `QueryExecutorNode` (re-executes queries from visualization artifacts)
- `ReadDataTool` (reads/executes existing insights via the AI assistant)
Both have `self._user` available but never pass it through, so when the `hogql-access-control` flag is enabled, queries hitting `system.*` ta

title: fix(phai): sse keepalive heartbeat and network error
body:
## Problem

Users see recurring "Oops something went wrong" in the AI chat due to transient network errors that should be retryable.

1. **No SSE keepalive**: Backend yields bytes only on real data. During long LLM/tool operations, the connection goes idle and gets killed by load balancers/proxies (typically 60s idle timeout).

2. **Incomplete retry logic**: Client only retries Chrome's "Failed to fetch". Firefox ("NetworkError when attempting to fetch resource.") and Safari ("Load failed"

title: fix: Disable alerts with invalid calculation intervals
body:
## Problem

Whilst looking into our alert slo metrics, I noticed that we recently received a fair amount of failures again. After some investigation, it turns out that MCP's patch certain alerts with the calculation interval set to `null`, this causes an alert in an invalid state and therefore causes a whole bunch of failure triggers as we can't schedule a new date for the alert.

Please refer to this notebook for all the details / statistics: https://us.posthog.com/project/2/notebooks/O5EP

title: fix: set COOP unsafe-none on /login when redirecting to OAuth flows
body:
## Problem

When a user initiates Vercel's "Link Existing Account" (connectable) flow, the popup opens on `us.posthog.com` (the configured Redirect URL). If the user isn't logged in on US - which is always the case for EU users since their account only exists on EU - the popup redirects through `/login?next=/connect/vercel/link?session=...`.

Django's default `Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin` on the `/login` page severs `window.opener`, so Vercel's parent window can no longer detect when

title: fix: add cross-region webhook proxy and lazy cleanup for orphaned Vercel integrations
body:
## Problem

Two issues with the Vercel integration:

1. **Cross-region deauthorization webhooks silently dropped**: All Vercel webhooks go to the US region. When a Vercel integration exists only on EU, the deauthorization webhook arrives at US, can't find the integration locally, and is silently logged as `vercel_webhook_deauthorize_unknown_config` without actually cleaning up the EU integration.

2. **Stale integrations block new connections**: If a Vercel integration is removed from Vercel's s

title: feat: add k8s-awareness crate for pod departure classification
body:
## Problem

PostHog's Kafka consumer rebalancing treats all pod departures the same, triggering full stop-the-world rebalances regardless of whether a pod left due to a rolling deploy, a downscale, or a crash. This causes unnecessary latency spikes and partition reassignment churn. To make smarter rebalancing decisions, the coordinator needs to understand _why_ a pod departed.

## Changes

- Add a new `k8s-awareness` crate under `rust/common/` that watches Kubernetes controllers (Deploymen

title: fix(visual-review): apply patches before VR submit
body:
VR was hashing the pre-patch PNGs from git checkout instead of the updated ones from the test shards. This meant VR never saw actual snapshot changes from the current CI run.

**Fix:** apply patches before VR runs in both storybook and playwright workflows. The commit-snapshots action now uses `git apply --check` to skip already-applied patches gracefully.

HogQL treats `current_date` (and similar keywords) as fields, causing resolution errors and blocking keyword usage.

title: fix: guard husky.sh source in git hooks to prevent worktree failures
body:
## Problem

Creating git worktrees fails with:

```
.husky/post-checkout: line 2: .husky/_/husky.sh: No such file or directory
```

The `post-checkout` hook unconditionally sources `.husky/_/husky.sh` on line 2, which doesn't exist in a fresh worktree before `pnpm install` runs. The hook fails before reaching the worktree-detection and bootstrap logic on lines 5–27.

This was introduced in #51104 (March 17) and breaks Claude Code worktree creation.

## Changes

Guard the `. husky.sh` source in a

We saw throttling on the CPU for the kafka-deduplicator due to two reasons:
- RocksDB Options were not wired properly to the column family, as they only applied to the default CF
- flush_wal is useless before checkpoint as checkpoint does it

## Changes

- Extract a shared `column_family_options()` function that applies all tuning (write buffers, compaction style, L0 triggers, target file size, compression, shared block cache) from `RocksDbConfig`

title: feat(flags): add auth token cache safety mechanisms
body:
## Problem

Long-TTL auth token cache entries (secret tokens, personal API keys, project secret API keys) rely on signal-based invalidation. If a signal is missed — due to a race condition, service restart, or bug — stale entries persist indefinitely, allowing revoked tokens to continue authenticating.

Additionally, when Celery retry tasks for cache invalidation exhaust all retries, the failure is silently swallowed with no alerting.

## Changes

- **Periodic verification task**: Scans all `pos

Nested exceptions can reuse the same stack frame raw ID. In the error tracking issue view, expanding one frame would also expand the matching frame in another exception because expansion state was keyed only by raw frame ID.

## Changes

- Scope frame expansion state by exception ID and frame raw ID instead of raw frame ID alone.
- Keep the change local to the stacktrace rendering path.

Came from a [report](https://posthoghelp.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/53800). Customers with non-UTC project timezones cannot load more grouped logs in CDP destination logs. Clicking "Load up to 10 older groups" does nothing because `toAbsoluteClickhouseTimestamp` formats the pagination cursor in UTC, but the HogQL backend interprets bare timestamps as being in the team's timezone. For a project in `America/Bogota` (UTC-5), this shifts the `date_to` cutoff 5 hours forward, making the

title: fix: node pre context when read from disk
body:
We have two ways of displaying stack traces in node:
- uploading source maps - this works fine,
- we also attempt to read code from the disk in an unminified form. If that succeeds, we attach special metadata to the event so then when cymbal resolves it, it uses that.

We had a bug in that second approach where order of lines was reversed :)

Code I changed only applies to reading context from the event (which reads it from the disk)

title: fix(replay): prevent rasterizer capture hang when ffmpeg dies
body:
## Summary

- When ffmpeg crashes, puppeteer-capture's `waitForTimeout()` hangs forever because it silently stops requesting frames without rejecting pending waits
- Races `waitForTimeout()` against a `captureStopped` event sentinel so the activity fails immediately instead of hanging until Temporal kills it
- Adds ffmpeg event logging (start command, stderr, errors) for diagnosing future failures

## Test plan

- [x] Validated on production — patched onto pods and confirmed the activity properl

title: perf(visual-review): replace pngjs with sharp for 6x faster hashing
body:
Replace pngjs (pure JS) with sharp (native libvips) for PNG decode. Add concurrent hashing (16 at a time).

**Before:** ~41s to hash 3083 storybook snapshots in CI
**After:** ~7s (6x faster)

Canonical RGBA pixel hashing preserved — identical visual content still produces identical hashes.

title: chore: tag cache_key in query log_comment
body:
## Problem

I noticed identical SQL queries re-execute on ClickHouse 1-3 seconds apart despite the cache staleness threshold being 5+ minutes and the execution mode being `blocking` (which means it goes to the cache first). 

https://metabase.prod-us.posthog.dev/question/2001-duplicate-blocking-queries

To diagnose whether this is caused by different cache keys vs cache write failures, we need the `cache_key visible in the query log.

## Changes

Adds `tag_queries(cache_key=cache_key)`

The `personhog/service` proto (the router's public API) had a transitive dependency on `personhog/leader` via the `UpdatePersonProperties` RPC.
This meant that any client generating code from the service proto (e.g. the Python `personhog_client`, or a future Node.js client) was forced to also generate leader code — even though clients never call the leader directly.

Additionally, the Python codegen script (`bin/generate_personhog_proto.sh`) was generating stubs for all protos u

title: fix: improve secret API key validation
body:
## Problem

`ProjectSecretAPIKeyAuthentication.find_secret_api_token` enforces the `phs_` prefix for tokens in the `Authorization` header but accepts any value from `data["secret_api_key"]` in the request body. This means an attacker can use a public project token (`phc_*`) — exposed in frontend JS snippets — to authenticate against secret-key-gated endpoints (`local_evaluation`, `remote_config`, `active_breakpoints`).

All three affected endpoints are read-only (GET), so the impact is data

title: feat(nodejs): personhog group reads
body:
## Problem

The nodejs ingestion/CDP services currently read person and group data directly from Postgres replicas.
We want to migrate group reads to the personhog gRPC service to reduce load on the persons database and consolidate data access behind a single service layer.
This needs to be rolled out incrementally with percentage-based traffic shifting and automatic fallback to Postgres on failure.

title: feat(flags): implement per-condition aggregation in Rust evaluation service
body:
## Problem

Feature flags currently support only a single aggregation mode per flag — either person-based or group-based. This means you can't create a flag that targets "all users in company X" (group) AND "specific users by email" (person) in the same flag. Users have to create multiple flags and manage them separately, which is error-prone and hard to reason about.

Ref: #33206 (design doc for mixed user + group targeting)

Builds on #52179, which added the per-condition `aggregation_group_ty

title: fix(workflows): Remove SES Send/Delivery webhook metrics to prevent triple counting email_sent
body: ## Problem Every email sent through workflows is counted 3x in the "Emails sent" metric. This happens because `email_sent` is recorded from three sources: once synchronously when the email is sent (`email.service.ts`), once from the SES `Send` webhook, and once from the SES `Delivery` webhook. A workflow that sends 6 emails shows 18 in the metrics.

title: fix(data-warehouse): restore Z suffix for Klaviyo datetime
body:
## Problem

Klaviyo data warehouse syncs configured as "append by date" re-import the entire dataset on every run instead of just new records, creating exact duplicates and inflating data warehouse usage.

The root cause is a regression introduced in #50629 (make Klaviyo source resumable, Mar 11). That PR branched from before #49890 (Z suffix fix, Mar 5) and rewrote `klaviyo.py` without carrying over the fix. This reverted `_format_incremental_value` to using `datetime.isoformat()`, which pr

title: fix(workflows): Remove direct pixel/redirect metric recording to prevent double counting with SES webhooks
body:
## Problem

After merging [#52210](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/52210), email open and click metrics are being double counted. Both the SES webhook handler and our custom tracking pixel/redirect handlers now record the same events, inflating the numbers (e.g. 42 opens shown for 24 emails sent).

This happens because SES fires its own Open/Click webhook events AND the email client loads our tracking pixel/follows our redirect - each path independently records a metric. On localhost

title: feat(experiments): throttle metric queries with rolling concurrency limit
body:
## Problem

Experiments fire all metric queries (sometimes up to 50) simultaneously via `Promise.all`. Each query goes through Celery's `process_query_task`, which has a per-team concurrency limit of 10 slots (Redis sorted set, no waiting queue). When all 50 hit at once, 40 get `ConcurrencyLimitExceeded` and enter a retry loop with exponential backoff. Meanwhile, the frontend's 606-second polling timeout ticks from when `pollForResults` starts - not from when Celery actually executes the query

title: fix(hogql): fix backslash escape parsing, add property-based tests
body:
## Problem

The HogQL SQL escape functions have hand-written example tests but no fuzz/property-based coverage. While adding property-based tests, we discovered a real (albiet not super interesting) bug: `replace_common_escape_characters` (in both Python and C++) used sequential `str.replace` calls that processed `\\` (escaped backslash) **last**. This meant `\\n` in escaped output was mis-parsed as `\` + newline instead of literal backslash + `n`.

For example, the string `\n` (a literal ba

title: feat(flags): optimize regex matching by pre-compiling builder
body:
## Problem

`match_property()` recompiles a `fancy_regex::Regex` from scratch on every evaluation of a `Regex`/`NotRegex` property filter. Regex compilation is orders of magnitude more expensive than matching.

This was identified in #52022 as the next optimization target: regex operators (~70us each) dominate total evaluation time at higher flag counts.

## Changes

Relocate regex compilation from the per-match hot path (`match_property()`) to a single upfront pass (`prepare_regexes()`)

## Problem

The flags HyperCache recently gained `evaluation_metadata` (dependency stages, missing deps, transitive deps), but the verification task (`verify_team_flags`) doesn't check for its presence. Cache entries written before this field was added silently lack it, leaving the Rust flags service without dependency information.

I recently ran `python manage.py warm_flags_cache` in prod-us and prod-eu, so I'm pretty confident every cache entry is good. But this would give me 100% confide

title: fix(toolbar): echo custom headers in CORS preflight for toolbar OAuth endpoints
body:
## Problem

Customers whose apps monkey-patch `window.fetch` to inject custom headers (e.g. `x-app-version`, `x-correlation-id`) cannot authenticate the toolbar. The toolbar runs inside the customer's page and uses `window.fetch` for the OAuth token exchange. When the injected headers aren't in PostHog's CORS `Access-Control-Allow-Headers`, the browser blocks the preflight and the token exchange fails with a network error.

This has been reported by multiple customers, particularly those usi

title: fix: add admin permission checks to billing-modifying endpoints
body:
## Problem

Member-level Vercel SSO users can call billing-modifying endpoints (activate, deactivate, patch limits, etc.). PostHog's `BillingViewset` had no role checks, so these requests pass through to the billing service which rejects them with 403 "permission_denied". This surfaces as a confusing 500 to the user. 132 occurrences in the last 7 days.

## Changes

Added `IsOrganizationAdmin` permission to all billing-modifying actions:
- `activate`, `deactivate`, `patch`, `subscription_switch_p

title: fix(flags): require evaluation_metadata in hypercache; remove dep graph fallback
body:
## Problem

During the rollout of `evaluation_metadata` in the hypercache, the Rust feature flags service had a fallback that reconstructed dependency graphs from flag definitions when `evaluation_metadata` was missing. Now that all cache entries written by Django include this field, the fallback is no longer needed and masks potential issues where stale or malformed cache entries silently degrade to less efficient evaluation paths.

## Changes

- Remove `#[serde(default)]` from `HypercacheFlags

title: fix(insights): skip redundant /insight_variables API calls on dashboard scroll
body:
## Problem

Each SQL insight card on a dashboard mounts its own keyed `variablesLogic` instance when it scrolls into view. Each instance's `afterMount` explicitly calls `getVariables()`, hitting `/api/environments/:team_id/insight_variables/` every time, even though the data lives in the shared singleton `variableDataLogic`. Scrolling a dashboard with many SQL tiles fires dozens of redundant identical requests.

title: fix: add cross-region proxy support to VercelProxyViewSet
body:
## Problem

The billing service calls `POST /api/vercel/proxy/` to forward usage/invoice data to Vercel. It routes based on `customer.license_id` (EU customers go to `eu.posthog.com`, US to `us.posthog.com`). But the proxy looks up `OrganizationIntegration` only in the local region's DB. If a customer's integration is in the other region, the proxy returns 404, causing "Vercel API call failed" errors (44+37+4 occurrences in the last week).

Other Vercel viewsets use `VercelRegionProxyMixin` for

title: fix(test): increase jest.setTimeout to 45s in postgres-parity tests
body:
<!-- mendral:session:action:01KK9DQ1GXJ4KJCMZRB6QY6F26 -->
## Summary

- Increases `jest.setTimeout` from 30s to 45s in `postgres-parity.test.ts` to give 15s of headroom above the `delayUntilEventIngested` polling budget (300 × 100ms = 30s).

## Context

Fixes a race condition introduced when PR #50006 reduced `maxDelayCount` from 1000→300 (capping the polling budget at 30s) without increasing `jest.setTimeout` to match. When Kafka/ClickHouse ingestion is slow, the polling exhausts its full 30s

title: fix: make Vercel connect flow work cross-region
body:
## Problem

The "Link Existing Account" flow in the Vercel Marketplace is broken for EU users. They see "Session expired or invalid".

The connect callback runs on US (Vercel's redirect URL points to `us.posthog.com`). It stores session data in **US Redis cache** and sets a signed **cookie on us.posthog.com**. When EU users get redirected to `eu.posthog.com` (where their project lives), neither the cache entry nor the cookie are available.

Reported in https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C043VJ93

title: fix(sessions): use range-based chunking for distinct_id in experimental backfill
body:
## Problem

The experimental sessions backfill chunks events by `cityHash64(distinct_id) % N`, but the events table ORDER BY includes `cityHash64(distinct_id)` as a sorted column:

```sql
ORDER BY (team_id, toDate(timestamp), event, cityHash64(distinct_id), cityHash64(uuid))
```

With modulo, matching values are uniformly scattered across the entire UInt64 range, so ClickHouse cannot use the primary index to skip granules — every chunk reads essentially all the data and filters at scan time. Wit

title: fix(cli,cymbal): create per-UUID ZIP for dSYM uploads
body:
## Problem

When a dSYM bundle contains multiple DWARF binaries (e.g. an Xcode debug dylib + app stub), the CLI zipped the entire bundle and shared that same blob across all UUIDs. On incremental rebuilds, stable UUIDs (like the app stub loader) got a new content hash whenever a sibling binary changed, causing `content_hash_mismatch` errors from the server.

## Changes

- **CLI (`cli/src/dsym/`)**: Each UUID now gets its own ZIP containing only its DWARF binary (+ optional source files), wrapped

**Problem:** 6e64ea2 consolidated `detect-snapshot-mode` into the `changes` job but accidentally dropped the `update-snapshots` label gate. This means every human PR was running in update mode — snapshot mismatches get silently auto-committed instead of failing the check.

title: fix(cohorts): add update_fields guard to all cohort post_save signal handlers
body:
## Problem

When a cohort recalculates, `_safe_save_cohort_state` calls `self.save()` without `update_fields`, triggering every `post_save` signal handler. This causes unnecessary cache invalidations (feature flag cache, dependency cache, hog function recompilation) on every recalculation cycle, even though only bookkeeping fields like `is_calculating` and `last_calculation` changed.

## Changes

- Define `COHORT_RECALCULATION_FIELDS` frozenset (`is_calculating`, `last_calculation`, `errors_calc

title: fix(cohorts):  self-heal stuck static cohorts and fix OOM in CH→PG sync
body:
## Problem

When a user duplicates a large dynamic cohort as a static cohort, the `insert_cohort_from_query` Celery task populates ClickHouse and then syncs to Postgres. For large cohorts (1M+ people), the PG sync step in `insert_cohort_people_into_pg` loads **all** person UUIDs from ClickHouse into a Python list at once, which OOMs the worker. Since SIGKILL bypasses Python's `finally` block, `is_calculating` is never reset to `False`.

Worse, `reset_stuck_cohorts` explicitly excludes static

title: feat(flags): preload referenced cohorts in flags hypercache

## Problem

The Rust feature-flags service queries PostgreSQL via `CohortCacheManager` for every team's cohorts on each `/flags` request, even though the cohort definitions rarely change. This adds unnecessary latency and PG load, especially for teams with many cohort-based flags.

## Changes

Preloads cohort definitions (including transitive dependencies) into the flags hypercache at cache-write time so the Rust service can skip the separate PG query.

**Python (cache write path):**
- Extracts

title: feat: (flags): optimize is_condition_match with zero-copy filtering

`is_condition_match()` performs two `iter().cloned().partition()` calls on every condition of every flag, cloning every `PropertyFilter` (which contains heap-allocated `String` and `serde_json::Value`) unnecessarily. All downstream consumers only need borrowed references.

_Note: see https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/issues/52017_

## Changes

Replace the clone-partition pattern with zero-copy filtered iteration. Filters are now evaluated in a single pass by branching on `pr

A customer reported their cohort showed stale data ("no new people since a day ago") despite `last_calculation` being recent. Removing and re-adding a filter fixed it by forcing a version bump.

title: fix(replay): use SECRET_KEY env var instead of DJANGO_SECRET_KEY
body:
## Summary

- Renames `DJANGO_SECRET_KEY` → `SECRET_KEY` in the recording-rasterizer config and dev startup script
- Companion to PostHog/charts PR for the Helm values fix

The posthog-app chart's ExternalSecret template creates the k8s secret key using the env var name (`DJANGO_SECRET_KEY`), but the deployment template mounts it using the property name (`SECRET_KEY`). This mismatch causes `CreateContainerConfigError` on all rasterizer pods. Using `SECRET_KEY` for both sides resolves it.

## Tes

title: chore(ci): auto-resolve outdated bot review comments
body:
## Problem

Bot review comments (Copilot, Greptile, etc.) pile up on PRs and remain unresolved even after the author addresses
them in follow-up commits. GitHub marks them "outdated" but never auto-resolves the threads, creating noise that
makes it harder to spot comments still needing attention.

## Changes

Adds a GitHub Actions workflow that runs on each PR push (`synchronize`) and auto-resolves outdated bot review
threads. Uses the GraphQL API (`resolveReviewThread` mutation) with n

## Problem

  Insights created or updated via the MCP server don't appear in the default Insights list view because the

  Closes [#41089](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/issues/41089)

`timeout-minutes` is not a valid key on jobs that call reusable workflows via `uses:`. GitHub Actions treats these as regular jobs when it sees `timeout-minutes`, then fails because `runs-on` is missing and `uses`/`with`/`secrets` are unexpected.

This is the same class of bug fixed in #51930 and #51832 for the Rust workflows — these four files were also affected.

## Changes

Remove invalid `timeout-minutes` from reusable workflow jobs in:
- `call-flags-project-board.yml`
- `ci-hobb

title: fix(error-tracking): set last_used on symbol set upload
body:
## Problem

Symbol sets created via the Django upload API never had `last_used` set, leaving it `NULL`. The Dagster cleanup job (`symbol_set_cleanup`) only garbage-collects symbol sets with a non-null `last_used` by default, so failed or incomplete uploads accumulated indefinitely.

## Changes

- Set `last_used = timezone.now()` when creating symbol sets in all upload paths:
  - `create_symbol_set()` (deprecated `create` and `start_upload` endpoints)
  - `bulk_create_symbol_sets()` (`bulk_start_

title: fix(metalytics): fix metalytics 5xxs
body:
## Problem

Metalytics endpoint is the single largest source of 504s across platform features endpoints. 

The metalytics frontend fires 3 requests simultaneously per page view:
- A `POST /metalytics/` (viewed metric) — **awaited** despite being fire-and-forget telemetry
- Two HogQL queries with `force_blocking` — each ties up a Django worker until ClickHouse returns

## Changes

- Switch both HogQL queries from `force_blocking` to `async` refresh — returns cached results instantly, re

title: chore(mcp): add feature/tool names validations
body:
## Problem

  MCP tool names had incomplete validation — only YAML-defined tools were checked for length (52 chars), while
  handwritten tools in JSON definitions and the runtime `TOOL_MAP` had no validation at all. There was also no
  character pattern validation anywhere, meaning invalid names could slip through and silently break in MCP
  clients like Cursor (which filters out tools exceeding its 60-char combined limit) or OpenAI API (which
  rejects names not matching `^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$

title: fix(alerts): add window param to all PyOD detectors to fix false positive anomaly alerts
body:
## Problem

PyOD-based anomaly detectors (isolation_forest, knn, lof, ocsvm, pca, ecod, copod, hbos) were firing false positive alerts because the alert checker fetched far too little historical data. The `DETECTOR_MIN_SAMPLES` guard (e.g. 10 for isolation forest) was accidentally the *only* thing driving the data fetch size, causing models to train on as few as 9 data points and producing garbage anomaly scores (score=1.0 on perfectly normal data).

Meanwhile, the simulation endpoint used the i

## Problem

The experimental sessions backfill splits each run into chunks using `team_id % N`. This produces uneven work distribution because some teams have far more events than others, meaning certain chunks process disproportionately more rows.

## Changes

- Add `distinct_id_chunks` config option that splits by `cityHash64(distinct_id) % N` instead
- This gives a much more even distribution since `cityHash64(distinct_id)` is already materialized in the events table ORDER BY key (so no extra

title: chore(devex): validate package.json script content in product lint
body:
**Problem**

`hogli product:lint` only checked that `backend:test` and `backend:contract-check` keys existed in `package.json` — it never looked at what they contained. This left a few dangerous failure modes undetected:

- A non-isolated product with `backend:contract-check` gets misclassified by `turbo-discover.js` as isolated, silently skipping the full Django test suite when that product changes
- A typo in the pytest path (e.g. `backend/typo_tests`) collects zero tests with no error
- A no-

title: fix(hogql): track used cte aliases
body:
# Problem

Queries that mixed `SELECT *` with additional unaliased expressions could fail in ClickHouse when the expanded `*` already exposed a generated column name matching the later expression.

In the reported case, a CTE produced an unaliased `toDate(period_end)` column, and the outer query also selected `toDate(period_end)`. Our ClickHouse printer auto-assigned the same synthetic alias to the later expression, which caused ClickHouse to reject the query because two different expression

title: fix(ci): add healthcheck to Elasticsearch service in docker-compose.base.yml
body:
<!-- mendral:session:action:01KM5CHT8NX64X7Q9XRXHGPTRR -->
## Summary

Fixes a recurring CI reliability issue where the `temporal` container fails to start because Elasticsearch isn't ready when Temporal tries to connect.

**Root cause:** The `elasticsearch` service had no healthcheck defined. Temporal's `depends_on` used `service_started` (container exists), not `service_healthy` (container is ready). On resource-constrained CI runners, Elasticsearch's JVM startup can be slow — the container ma

title: chore: wire worker_threads var to kafka-deduplicator
body:
## Problem

The kafka-deduplicator's `worker_threads` config (default: 4) was declared but never wired into the tokio runtime. The `#[tokio::main]` macro defaults to CPU core count, making the config ineffective. Additionally, RocksDB batch read/write operations ran directly on the async runtime, risking tokio worker thread starvation and causing random liveness probe failures.

## Changes

- Replace `#[tokio::main]` with an explicit `tokio::runtime::Builder` that uses `config.worker_threa

title: fix(limiters): replace fixed sleeps with polling in rate limiter integration tests
body:
**Problem**
All integration tests in `global_rate_limiter_integration_tests.rs` use fixed sleeps (300-500ms) before asserting on Redis state. The background pipeline flush can take up to ~800ms under CI load (100ms tick + 500ms write timeout + task startup latency), causing intermittent failures.

**Fix**
Replace all 14 fixed-sleep sites with a `poll_assert!` macro that retries every 100ms up to a 3s deadline. Exits as soon as the condition is met (typically ~200-300ms), so no slowdown in the ha

title: fix: make Vercel installation DELETE idempotent
body:
## Problem

When a customer's `OrganizationIntegration` record is already deleted (or was never created), Vercel's `DELETE /api/vercel/v1/installations/{id}` call returns 500. This happens because:

1. US doesn't find the installation, proxies to EU
2. EU doesn't find it either, raises `NotFound` from `_handle_missing_installation` inside the region proxy mixin's `dispatch` override
3. Since this runs before `super().dispatch()`, DRF's exception handler never catches it - Django treats it as an

Currently, when backfilling precalculated person properties, events are emitted to Kafka with a key set to the distinct_id. This causes all events for a particular distinct_id to be routed to the same partition, which can lead to uneven partition distribution and potential performance issues.

## Changes

- Events will now be distributed across partitions in round-robin fashion instead of being

## Problem

`fetch_and_locally_cache_all_relevant_properties` is a 260+ line function that ran three database queries **sequentially on a single connection** from the `persons_reader` pool:

https://posthoghelp.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/53704 - we weren't showing number fields as an option for Snowflake incremental syncs.

This is because in Snowflake's information schema, all numeric/number types get reported as `number` for their `DATA_TYPE` - see https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-numeric#decimal-dec-numeric.

## Changes

`numeric` -> `number`

The kafka-deduplicator service had three operational issues in production:

1. WARN log spam ("Failed to update metadata.json after commit") caused by a race condition between `update_metadata_for_partition` and `cleanup_store_files` during Kafka rebalances
2. Pods terminating with `Error` status but no visible error logs, because panics weren't captured by the structured JSON logger

Customers using Entra ID with nested groups get 400 errors in their provisioning logs. When a parent group contains nested sub-groups, Entra ID sends the nested group's SCIM ID as a member reference. 
The `User.objects.get(id=<uuid>)` throws a `ValueError` (not `User.DoesNotExist`) because user.id is an integer field. 

[More context](https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C09B6HYA6Q5/p1773326607369709?thread_ts=1762279729.310209&cid=C09B6HYA6Q5).

title: fix(experiments): set explicit query limit for experiment breakdowns

## Problem

https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C0441QU36RL/p1773627916502409

Experiment breakdown queries don't set an explicit LIMIT. The HogQL executor injects `LIMIT 100` by default, silently truncating results for high-cardinality breakdowns like pathname. This produces incorrect exposure counts.

## Changes

Set an explicit `LIMIT 50000` (`MAX_SELECT_RETURNED_ROWS`) on the outer experiment query.

## How did you test this code?

Existing breakdown tests pass.

title: fix(error-tracking): use decompressed byte count for cymbal cache memory accounting
body:
## Problem

After zstd compression was added to the symbol_data container format (#51484), cymbal's symbol set cache started tracking **compressed** byte sizes while storing **decompressed and parsed** objects. This caused the cache to underestimate actual memory usage by 5-20x, preventing eviction from triggering in time and leading to OOM events.

## Changes

- Added `read_symbol_data_with_byte_count` to the `posthog-symbol-data` crate, which returns the decompressed payload size alongside the

title: feat(flags): skip PostgreSQL fallback for team token lookups
body:
## Problem

We're seeing a huge volume of requests for a project token that doesn't exist in our database, putting unnecessary load on PostgreSQL. We have a negative cache for invalid tokens, but it can only do so much — each cache eviction still triggers a PG query for a token that will never resolve. We could enlarge the cache and extend the TTL, but that would only make us more susceptible to cache poisoning.

As HyperCache (Redis/S3) matures as the source of truth for team tokens, we no

title: chore(personhog): gRPC connection churn tuning
body:
## Problem

The personhog Django client shows p95 latency of ~80ms while the service itself processes requests in ~5ms.
The root cause is gRPC channel churn: each Django worker process (Nginx Unit, 4 workers/pod, 30-200 pods) holds a singleton `PersonHogClient`, each worker's channel goes IDLE between requests. Every subsequent request pays the full reconnection cost. (DNS resolve + TCP connect + HTTP/2 handshake = ~60-80ms overhead).

Channel state transition  IDLE→CONNECTING rate matches

title: chore(auth): remove team access cache warming system
body:
## Problem

The `/flags/definitions` auth cache eagerly warmed Redis for every team on a 10-minute periodic task, even though only ~25% of teams use local evaluation. Most teams use only one key at a time, yet warming was also triggered on every personal API key change regardless of whether that key was ever used for local evaluation.

This added unnecessary Redis writes, database queries, and Celery task overhead with no measurable benefit.

I plan to go a different direction with this th

title: chore(devex): add tach boundary check to product lint
body:
tach has no built-in way to detect undeclared modules — if a new Python package appears in `products/` without a `[[modules]]` entry in `tach.toml`, it can import anything freely with zero enforcement.

**What changed**

Merged the old `TachInterfacesCheck` (isolated-only) into a single `TachCheck` that runs for all products with Python files. It first verifies a `[[modules]]` entry exists, then checks for `interfaces` on isolated products.

Also registered the 7 products that were missing from

title: fix(personhog-replica): rm test before acquire
body:
## Problem

The `personhog_replica_db_pool_acquire_duration_ms` p99 metric shows a ~5ms baseline with frequent spikes to 30ms+. Every `pool.acquire()` call sends a health-check ping through PgBouncer to Postgres before returning the connection, adding a full network roundtrip to every single query.

With `test_before_acquire: true`, sqlx pings every connection before returning it from the pool. The ~5ms baseline *is* that ping roundtrip through PgBouncer. The 30ms+ spikes are variance in tha

title: chore(ci): add timeout-minutes to all CI jobs
body:
## Problem

GitHub Actions jobs default to a [6-hour execution timeout](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/actions-limits). Without explicit `timeout-minutes`, a stalled job (hung test, stuck Docker pull, deadlocked process) silently burns CI minutes for hours before GitHub kills it. Many of our CI workflow jobs were missing this setting entirely.

## Changes

1. **Adds `timeout-minutes` to every job** across all workflow files (~120 additions across 63 files). All values were validate

title: feat(flags): add in-process group type cache
body:
## Problem

The feature-flags Rust service fetches group type mappings from PostgreSQL on ~10% of total requests (`posthog_grouptypemapping` table). In production (prod-us), this generates **~1,300 DB queries/sec** across all pods. The data does not change often (only when a team creates a new group type, which is essentially a one-time setup operation), making it an ideal candidate for in-process caching.

While p50 latency is ~0.67ms, p99 reaches ~8.3ms on most pods, with tail latencies sp

title: fix(error-tracking): skip property values API fetch for assignee properties
body:
## Problem

`PropertyValue` has two `useEffect`s that call `loadPropertyValues` whenever `propertyOptions` hasn't been loaded yet. For assignee properties (`PropertyType.Assignee`, e.g. `resource/assignee`), there is no backend endpoint at `api/resource/values`; assignee data is sourced from `membersLogic`/`rolesLogic` instead. This caused an infinite request loop: the call errors → status becomes `"error"` → effect re-fires → repeat.

## Changes

- Added `!isAssigneeProperty` guard to bot

title: ci(frontend): add products/**/*.json to path filter to catch oxfmt violations in PRs

## Summary

- Adds `products/**/*.json` to the `dorny/paths-filter` in `ci-frontend.yml` so that PRs adding or modifying JSON files under `products/` trigger the `frontend-format` job (oxfmt check).
- Without this, JSON-only changes in `products/` bypassed the formatting check entirely on PRs — the check only ran on master push, which is how the unformatted workflow templates in #50184 slipped through.

Closes insight: https://app.mendral.com/insights/01KM2TYZH5CPAB6D6RRCEYRCCA

title: chore(personhog_client): more gRPC client metrics
body:
## Problem

The Django gRPC personhog client had limited observability — only request duration and a status-labeled request counter. When latency spikes occurred, we couldn't easily distinguish between:

- Timeout/deadline exceeded errors vs other failures
- Connection establishment problems (slow DNS, network issues between Django and the router)
- Channel instability (frequent reconnects, prolonged TRANSIENT_FAILURE states)

The existing `grpc.enable_retries` channel option was also a

title: chore(personhog): instrument connection level behavior
body:
## Problem

The personhog services (router and replica) only had request-level metrics (`grpc_server_requests_total`, `grpc_server_request_duration_ms`). We had no visibility into connection-level behavior or request concurrency, making it hard to diagnose issues like connection storms, pool backpressure, or in-flight request buildup.

## Changes

- **Connection tracking:** Added `TrackedTcpStream` wrapper and `tracked_tcp_incoming` helper that tracks active gRPC server connections via a `

title: chore(ci): add timeout and partial clone to bundle size job
body:
## Problem

The frontend bundle size CI job can hang indefinitely (observed 35+ min) when `compressed-size-action` runs `git fetch -n origin master:master` on a degraded runner. The job has no timeout, so it burns runner minutes for up to 6 hours (GitHub Actions default).

## Changes

- Add `timeout-minutes: 10` to the `frontend-bundle-size` job (normal runtime is ~4 min)
- Add `filter: blob:none` to the checkout step so the action's internal `git fetch` only pulls commit/tree objects, not blobs

As of https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/51083, the Rust feature-flags service currently rebuilds the entire dependency graph from scratch on every request using petgraph's Kahn's algorithm. title: fix: don't block org deletion retry when user already deleted
body:
## Problem

When a user deletes their organization and then immediately deletes their account (enabled by #50875), the async org deletion task can fail on retry. 

The task requires the initiating user to exist, but if they've already deleted their account, retries raise `ValueError("Cannot delete team data: user not found")` and never reach the org/project deletion step. 

This results in orphaned org and project records in Postgres, all the actual data (events, session replays, persons, etc.)

Didn't realize this. With that in mind, I think we should standarize on sending `device_id` only through person properties. For now though, we need to check both places.

body:
## Problem

Real users hit multi-GB tab memory and browser OOM crashes after extended
PostHog use — heap snapshots and Chrome's task manager have shown 11 GB on a
single tab. The leak is in the renderer process: detached React trees pile up
across SPA navigations and never get released.

Root cause is Chromium's `DocumentTimeline`. It holds a strong reference to
every running animation, and each animation holds its target element via
`KeyframeEffect`. When a component with an `animation: ... inf

```python
if session is not None:

else:

```

title: chore: discourage python inline imports in AGENTS.md
body:
## Problem

`AGENTS.md` doesn't call out a Python style point that comes up in review: imports inside functions/methods. Inline imports are not best practice — they hide dependencies from static analysis, add lookup overhead on hot paths, and tend to mask circular-import issues rather than fix them.

## Changes

Add a single bullet under `Code Style` clarifying that imports belong at module level, with the only acceptable exceptions being unavoidable circular imports and `TYPE_CHECKING` blocks.

title: feat(error-tracking): add HTTP webhook sub-template for issue spiking

In Error tracking → Configuration → Alerting, the destination list is built by iterating each Hog function template across the three error tracking sub-template IDs (`issue created`, `issue reopened`, `issue spiking`). HTTP Webhook had sub-template entries for `issue created` and `issue reopened`, but not `issue spiking`, so customers could wire up webhook notifications for the first two triggers but not the third. Discord, Microsoft Teams, and Slack support all three.

title: chore(batch-exports): limit concurrent backfills
body:
## Problem

There is currently no limit on how many batch export backfills a single team can have running simultaneously. A team can fire off arbitrarily many backfills, each of which puts pressure on shared infrastructure (notably ClickHouse memory and execution time), risking degraded performance for other batch exports across all teams.

We want a cheap, immediate guardrail at the API layer so a single team cannot accidentally (or otherwise) consume an outsized share of shared resources v

title: fix(retention): keep first-ever cohort intact when applying a breakdown
body:
## Problem

Retention insights using **First time ever** plus a **breakdown** can produce per-bucket cohort sums that exceed the unbroken total. A user whose absolute-first start event predates the breakdown property (e.g. an event captured before a feature flag rolled out) ends up double-counted: once in the empty-string bucket on their true first-event day, and again in a tagged bucket on the day their first flag-tagged event landed.

https://posthoghelp.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/57068

## Changes

Adds a single `actions/cache/save@v4` step in the migration-check job (master push only) that caches `schema.sql.gz` under `posthog-schema-master-${{ github.sha }}`.

No consumer in this PR — that's deliberate. Landing the pr

title: perf(insights): memoize TrendsTooltip render-derived values
body:
## Problem

`TrendsTooltip` recomputes `seriesData`, `renderCount`, and `renderSeries` on every parent render, and passes a fresh `() => {}` as the `onClose` fallback when no pin is held. Each of those is enough to bust `InsightTooltip`'s memoization, so the tooltip rerenders on every parent identity churn even when the underlying values haven't changed.

## Changes

- `seriesData` → `useMemo` keyed on `[context.seriesData, context.dataIndex]`.
- `renderCount` → `useCallback` keyed on `[showPerc

title: feat(experiments): gate precomputation by 12h minimum runtime
body:
## Problem

When an experiment is freshly launched, the exposure precomputation system caches the today-window result for 15 minutes (per `DEFAULT_EXPOSURE_TTL_SECONDS["0d"]`). For a brand-new experiment all of its data lives in that today window, so newly-arriving exposures stay invisible until the cache TTL elapses — refreshing the results page returns the same stale snapshot for up to 15 minutes. This is most painful right after launch, when users typically refresh often to verify ramp-up a

title: fix(llma): return all spans for a trace, regardless of duration
body:
## Problem

The trace detail page (`/llm-analytics/traces/<id>`) and the same trace expanded from `/llm-analytics/sessions` could show different sets of spans for the same `trace_id`. The detail page applied a tight ~30 minute window around the URL `timestamp` parameter (frontend `+10 min` on `date_to`, backend `±10 min` buffer), while sessions expansion sent no `dateRange` and got the 7 day default. Long-running agent traces and late-arriving spans got cut off on the detail page only.

To custo

When a `cdp-events-consumer` `backgroundTask` does not resolve before the consumer rebalance timeout, librdkafka force-recovers and abandons in-flight offsets, leading to message replay. Today we only have one outer histogram covering the whole leg, so a hang doesn't tell us which sub-component is responsible.

## Changes

Wraps each leg of the backgroundTask `Promise.all` in `instrumentFn` so per-leg duration shows up on the existing `instrumented_function_duration_seconds{fun

title: feat(observability): add per-request pg/ch query count and max to Server-Timing
body:
## Problem

The `Server-Timing` header on api responses already includes `pg=<ms>` for total `cursor.execute()` wall-clock on a request. That single number can't disambiguate between:

- **N+1 chain** — many small queries each paying a pgbouncer round-trip
- **One fat query** — a single SELECT taking seconds
- **Pool exhaustion** — many fast queries waiting on transaction-pool checkout

You see them all as 'pg=4000' and have to guess.

## Changes

`QueryCounter` now also tracks per-request:
- `c

title: fix(flags): use correct --retry-count flag for gh api
body:
## Problem

Hwhoops!

PR #56920 introduced `gh api --retry 3` in the canary flags workflow, but `gh api` has no `--retry` flag. The workflow would fail on every PR synchronize event when it tries to read `state.yaml` from PostHog/charts.

## Changes

Change `--retry 3` to `--retry-count 3`, the actual flag name (added in gh CLI 2.55.0). GitHub-hosted runners currently ship gh 2.89.0, well above the minimum.

`--retry-count` retries on transient HTTP 5xx and network errors, not on 4xx r

title: feat(logs): add metrics for alert processing durations and pending alerts
body:
## Problem

Observability gaps in the logs alerting pipeline made it difficult to diagnose latency and backlog issues. Specifically, there was no visibility into how long alerts waited on the concurrency semaphore, how long ClickHouse queries took per evaluation, how long Postgres writes took when saving alert state, or how many due alerts remained pending at the end of a cycle.

## Changes

Four new metrics have been added to the logs alerting pipeline:

- **`logs_alerting_semaphore_wait_ms`**

Each mousemove mutated `style.left` on the div, forcing the browser to invalidate style and commit layout for the chart's subtree. With more data drawn on the surrounding canvas, the cost of that per-event commit grew — measurable in the chart-bench scene as roughly 60% of the hog-vs-chart.js hover frame-wait gap at 100K points (around 100K

## Problem

Editing a dashboard layout (moving or resizing tiles) returns a generic 500 with no useful error body for a meaningful share of dashboard edits across many users. ## Problem

The canary feature-flags workflow had a bug where `pull_request: synchronize` events (new commits pushed to a PR with an active canary) hardcoded `targetEnvironment='dev'` and `canaryWeight='auto'`. If the PR's canary was running in prod-us or prod-eu at a specific weight, pushing a new commit would silently overwrite it: ArgoCD would prune the prod canary deployment and replace it with a dev canary at auto weight.

Since Modal snapshots are not supported in the EU region, we need to have a way of resuming cloud runs from the last checkpoint.

This adds support for that behind an env var, because of the way we’re resuming cloud runs, this walks the chain of previous cloud runs to pull the logs for the current run, so that the last saved checkpoint is always included.

It also adds some logs for debugging cloud run resuming.

## Problem

Typing a large number into the points box in the chart-bench scene blew up Chart.tsx with `RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded`. Root cause: `Math.min(...allValues)` / `Math.max(...allValues)` in the y-domain computation passes every data value as a separate function argument, which overflows the JS engine's argument-count limit (around 100k on most engines). Same pattern existed in `scales.ts` for the log-scale path. Any real insight with a large enough series would hit it

The root cause is in capture: distinct_id is assigned once per OTLP request from resource attributes only. A single batch carrying spa

title: fix(llma): make trace tree and event content scroll independently

## Problem

The LLM analytics trace viewer used a single page-level scroll for the whole scene. When a trace has many spans/generations, the tree on the left grew tall and pushed the selected event's input/output far down the page. To inspect an event, users had to scroll back up to the right pane, then back down to click another node in the tree — repeating constantly while exploring a long agent trace.

Reported via in-app bug ticket.

title: refactor(api): hoist project_id/environment_id/organization_id into components.parameters
body:
## Problem

drf-spectacular inlines path parameters into every operation that uses them. ``project_id`` alone shows up ~1100 times in the generated spec, with byte-identical schema and description each pass. ``environment_id`` adds another ~440. ``organization_id`` another 79. Repeating identical parameter blobs ~1,680 times bloats the spec, makes it harder to maintain (one source of truth per param), and lights up Vacuum's ``description-duplication`` rule for the same reason.

## Changes

Hoist

title: fix(api): emit OpenAPI 3.0 valid spec, add consistency lint
body:
## Problem

The generated OpenAPI spec at `frontend/tmp/openapi.json` violates the OpenAPI 3.0 grammar in several places. Strict linters like Vacuum flag these correctly; downstream codegen tools are forgiving but produce lower-quality output. drf-spectacular itself doesn't validate cross-field consistency (e.g. `default ∈ enum`), so spec bugs slip through `--fail-on-warn` until something breaks.

## Changes

Three groups of fixes in `posthog/api/documentation.py`, plus an automatic gate so this

title: fix(dagster): surface incomplete state in part breaker on failure
body:
## Problem

`_ensure_staging_table` returned early if the staging table already existed, never checking whether its schema still matched the source. When source picks up new columns via `ALTER TABLE`, the staging table doesn't inherit them, and `ATTACH PARTITION FROM` later raises `Code 122: Tables have different structure`, after an `INSERT SELECT` has already run.

The pipeline mutates the source table at two points: `ATTACH PARTITION FROM` (Step 8) attaches the new smaller parts, and `ALT

The MCP Cloudflare Worker is producing persistent error log spam:

```
[API] Permission denied on GET /api/projects/{id}/groups_types/: API key missing required scope 'group:read'
```

This affects users whose OAuth tokens were issued before `group:read` was added to `OAUTH_SCOPES_SUPPORTED` (March 24, commit b2134439). Refreshed tokens inherit the original grant's scopes, so affected users keep getting 403s on the groups_types endpoint until their 30-day refresh token expires and th

title: fix(mcp): remove 'introspection' from OAUTH_SCOPES_SUPPORTED
body:
## Problem

Closes #55307.

`mcp.posthog.com/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource` advertises `introspection` in `scopes_supported`, but the authorization server at `oauth.posthog.com/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server` does not recognize it as a grantable scope.

Per [RFC 9728](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9728), spec-compliant MCP clients (including Claude Code) read `scopes_supported` from the protected resource and include every listed scope in the `authorize` request. The au

title: perf(hog-charts): split hover from static layer onto a separate canvas
body:
## Problem

In hog-charts, every mousemove repainted the entire chart. \`hoverIndex\` was a dep of the single \`useChartDraw\` effect, so on hover we'd \`clearRect\` the whole canvas and re-trace every series's polyline plus all areas/points before drawing the highlight ring on top.

At small datasets this is invisible (the rAF frame budget dominates). At larger datasets it's ugly: a 30-move hover sweep over a 10-series × 100k-point chart took ~1.5 s — visibly janky and far slower than chart.js

body:
## Problem

The SQL alias `main_covariate_sum_product` (used in CUPED-enabled mean metric queries) carries a `main_` prefix that doesn't mean anything — there's no corresponding "non-main" covariate sum product. The other CUPED columns are named `covariate_sum` and `covariate_sum_squares`, so the prefix here is the odd one out.

## Changes

Rename `main_covariate_sum_product` → `covariate_sum_product` everywhere it appears:

- SQL alias in both branches (regular + winsorized) of the mean metric

title: fix(ci): shadow test selection breaks on large PRs
body:
## Problem

The shadow test selection workflow started failing on master with `Argument list too long` errors when bash tried to start any post-compute step. Root cause: the workflow piped the full Snob+AST result JSON through `steps.selection.outputs.result`, which Actions re-injects as an env var on every later step. On PRs touching many files the blob blew past `ARG_MAX` and the runner couldn't even spawn `/usr/bin/bash`.

## Changes

- Workflow no longer round-trips the JSON through step out

title: fix(insights): allow hogql breakdowns on trends with data warehouse series
body:
## Problem

Trends insights with a `DataWarehouseNode` series rejected `hogql`-type breakdowns with the error:

> Event based breakdowns are not supported for trends insights with a data warehouse series.

But a HogQL breakdown isn't event-based — `parse_expr` resolves the expression against the FROM clause, which for a data warehouse series is the warehouse table itself. So a query like a breakdown by `status` on a `zendesk_tickets` series is well-formed and should be allowed.

`pos

title: chore(replay): bound set based metrics to avoid runaway payload sizes
body:
## Problem

In the session-feature-recorder, set based metrics, especially the set of visited urls caused runaway payload sizes that failed to produce.

## Changes

uniqifies URLs using md5 and caps the max amount of unique set entries per recorder instance to `MAX_UNIQUE_VALUES = 1000`. This way the payload will never exceed 50KB which is below 1MB.

## How did you test this code?

Unit tests.

grab a real request/response pair for use as a test fixture when an upstream
misbehaves. We also can't enforce a uniform transport because nothing
prevents a new source from dropping in `requests.get(...)` directly.

title: fix(logs-ingestion): limit to 50 concurrent messages processing at a time

when we have large batches doing lots of work we get kafka produce timeouts, presumably because the kafka produce call gets cpu starved and never given the chance to finish

title: feat(phai): add v1->v2 tool deprecation redirects in MCP exec dispatcher
body:
## Problem

PostHog MCP v2 (single-exec) drops 6 tools that were available in v1 by marking them `new_mcp: false` in `services/mcp/schema/tool-definitions.json`. When the model calls one via `exec call <tool>`, `findTool` throws a generic `Unknown tool: "X". Available tools: ...` error that dumps the entire 270-tool catalog. The model gets no signal that the tool was *intentionally removed* nor what to use instead, so it tends to retry the same call or pick an arbitrary nearby tool.

## Changes

None of the buttons in the scene carry `data-attr` values, so we can't track usage via autocapture.

title: fix(dagster): use ATTACH PARTITION FROM instead of per-part ATTACH in…
body:
… part breaker

## Problem

The part breaker uses per-part `ALTER TABLE ... ATTACH PART '<name>'` to move parts from staging into the source table. CH silently skips the attach when the part's block range doesn't fit existing state ([ClickHouse PR 97040](https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/pull/97040)). When staging produces merged parts with block ranges that don't align with the source table's partition state, those parts are silently dropped, requiring manual recovery.

## Changes

```
m                 = { default: ModuleNamespace }
m.default         = { default: ReactJson }    ← still a Module-like object
m.default.default = ReactJson (

The tracing spans API (`POST/GET` under `tracing/spans/*`) was returning **500 Internal Server Error** when loading traces in the app. The failure was **`UntaggedQueryError`**: ClickHouse `sync_execute` requires `product` and `feature` query tags (enforced in dev/CI for observability and routing), but the tracing `SpansViewSet` never set them. Other products that run HogQL against ClickHouse (e.g. logs) already call `tag_queries` at the start of the request handler.

Gemini's Files API stores uploaded files for 48h. Per-request cleanup in the
session-summary workflow is best-effort, so any path that bypasses it leaves
files behind until TTL — eventually pushing against the 20 GB project quota.

## Changes

A 30-min Temporal Schedule lists Gemini files older than 4h, asks Temporal
whether the owning workflow is terminal/missing, and deletes only those.
Uploads carry the workflow id in `display_name`, deployment-prefixed so a
shared Gemini API key

Direct postgres/duckdb sources support `FROM range(10)` and `FROM generate_series(1, 10)` because both have a hand-rolled `FunctionCallTable` subclass (`RangeTable`, `GenerateSeriesTable`) explicitly registered in the database root. This could leave PII in stored log attributes when `pii_scrub_logs` is on.

In single-exec mode, the MCP server registers a single `exec` tool that dispatches to all the underlying PostHog MCP tools via `exec call <tool> <json>`. mcpcat's tracing only sees the outer `exec` invocation at the MCP protocol layer — the inner tool dispatch is just an in-process function call and never produces its own analytics event.

## Changes

- Added `MCP_TOOL_CALLED = 'mcp_tool_called'` to `AnalyticsEvent` (name aligned with mcpcat's tool-call convention).

title: feat(logs): bounded concurrency for alert eval loop
body:
## Problem

Sequential alert evaluation in the logs alerting Temporal activity was overrunning the 60-second cron interval when the alert population grew beyond ~40 alerts. Each per-alert evaluation blocks on a ClickHouse query taking roughly 1.3–3.7 seconds, making purely sequential execution a bottleneck that worsens linearly with alert count.

## Changes

Alert evaluation in `check_alerts_activity` now runs with bounded concurrency using `asyncio.TaskGroup` and a `asyncio.Semaphore`. The setu

title: fix(ws): add HTTP retry for rate-limited data import sources
body:
## Problem

Data warehouse Zendesk imports fail permanently on HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) errors because the shared REST client has no per-request retry logic. When Zendesk rate-limits after ~2 pages of ticket_events, the activity crashes, Temporal restarts the whole import from scratch (since incrementality is disabled for this endpoint), and it hits the same rate limit at roughly the same point

## Changes

  Shared REST client retry (rest_client.py):
  - Added RESTClientRetryableErro

title: feat(logs): add count ranges endpoint for adaptive bucket counts
body:
## Problem

Agents querying logs currently have no cheap way to find *when* within a time window log volume is concentrated. The existing `logs-count` tool returns a scalar total, and `logs-sparkline-query` returns bucketed data but without explicit `date_from`/`date_to` per bucket — making it awkward for an agent to drill into a sub-range without reasoning about interval arithmetic. This gap forces agents to either over-fetch with `query-logs` or make imprecise follow-up calls.

title: fix(auth): skip known-device cookie for InternalAPIUser
body:
## Problem

`KnownLoginDeviceCookieMiddleware` (added in #55093) calls `set_known_device_cookie(response, request.user)` on every authenticated response, which formats the cookie name with [user.id](http://user.id). When a request is authenticated via `InternalAPIAuthentication`, `request.user` is an `InternalAPIUser` (with pk = -2) and no id attribute. So the middleware crashes with AttributeError: `InternalAPIUser object has no attribute id` and every internal-API endpoint returns 500.

Affect

This forces clients that have otherwise standardized on the multi-breakdown shape to special-case data warehouse trends.

title: feat(data-warehouse): warn when incremental field has no index on source
body:
## Problem

When a user picks an incremental field during data warehouse source setup, every sync issues `SELECT … WHERE <field> >= <last_max>`. If that field isn't the leading column of an index (or the engine equivalent — clustering / sort / partition key), each sync becomes a full table scan: slow, prone to timing out, and rough on the source DB. Today the user gets no signal until they hit the issue in production. The only existing per-field warning in the sync method modal is for nullable

The existing `if not res:` guard only catches a falsy top-level body, not an empty results array, so the access on `res["results"][0]["updatedAt"]` crashes the `import_data_activity_sync` Temporal activity for the af

HTTPError: 401 Client Error: Unauthorized for url: https://api.clerk.com/v1/users?l

surfaced from the Stripe source and other data-import sources running through

```python
stats_start_idx = 1 + num_breakdowns
num_samples = result[stats_start_idx]
sum_value   = result[stats_start_idx + 1]
sum_squares = result[stats_start_idx + 2]
```

title: feat(logs): switch to stateless bucketed alert evaluation
body:
## Problem

Alert evaluation runs a single ClickHouse count per check and stores the result in a Postgres `LogsAlertEvent` (CHECK kind) row. The N-of-M evaluator reads the last M historical rows back from Postgres on every tick to reconstruct its decision window.

This means:

- Two Postgres round-trips per alert tick (insert the new row, read the last M-1).
- An inline pruning loop on every eval to keep the table bounded.
- Query windows anchored on wall-clock `now`, so scheduler lag varies the

title: chore(insights): remove PostHog leaks from hog-charts and tidy public API

The hog-charts library is meant to be PostHog-agnostic — the README rules say no kea, no PostHog imports, no PostHog class names. The architecture review found two leaks (`resolveVariableColor` import in `ReferenceLine`, hardcoded `InsightTooltipWrapper--pinned` class in `Tooltip`) plus a few public-API gaps that forced consumers to deep-import. This PR clears both.

## Changes

- `overlays/ReferenceLine.tsx`: drops the `resolveVariableColor` import. Colors flow straight to inline DO

title: feat(insights): remove autocapture-save-as-action flag gate
body:
## Problem
