title: fix(stripe-app): add CSP trailing slashes and fallback for environment.constants
body:
## Problem

Two bugs in the published Stripe app, both flagged in the production app's DevTools console:

### 1. CSP `connect-src` blocks every API call

Per the [CSP3 spec](https://www.w3.org/TR/CSP3/#match-url-to-source-expression), source expressions without a trailing slash match only the literal resource. `https://us.posthog.com/api` matches `/api` and nothing else - it does not match `/api/projects/.../query/`. Every API call from the dashboard app is blocked:

```
Fetch API cannot load ht

title: fix(integrations): use stripe apps oauth token endpoint
body:
## Problem

The Stripe integration's OAuth token-exchange has been hitting the wrong endpoint since it shipped. PR #49572 wired `kind="stripe"` token exchange against `https://connect.stripe.com/oauth/token` (Stripe **Connect** OAuth, used by platforms onboarding sub-accounts). But the authorize URL we use is `marketplace.stripe.com/oauth/v2/authorize`, which is **Stripe Apps OAuth**, a different system. Codes minted by `marketplace.stripe.com` cannot be redeemed at the Connect endpoint. Stripe

title: fix(data-warehouse): redact source fields marked secret regardless of input type
body:
## Problem

Until now, redaction of source credentials in API responses was driven entirely by the `SourceFieldInputConfigType.PASSWORD` rendering type. Source fields that need a multi-line input (PEM-encoded private keys, mTLS certificates, etc.) had to use `TEXTAREA`, and as a result they were returned in plain text from the external data source endpoint to anyone with read access to the source.

The leak surfaces in two places today:

- **Snowflake** keypair auth — `private_key`
- **Temporal.

title: fix(logs): snap alert NextCheckAt timestamps to the canonical cadence grid
body:
## Problem

Alert scheduling had a sub-minute drift bug: when `advance_next_check_at` returned a timestamp with a non-zero seconds component (e.g. `12:05:30`), the scheduler cron (which fires every minute) would skip a full tick waiting for it, causing the alert to be picked up ~30 seconds late every single cycle. Additionally, alerts created at different times would land on different per-creation-time grids, meaning two 5-minute alerts could fire at `:02/:07/:12` and `:04/:09/:14` instead of sh

title: fix(scenes): drop leaking warm-mount in sceneLogic.loadScene
body:
## Problem

Every scene navigation leaks +1 mount on the destination scene's kea logic.

`sceneLogic.loadScene` does a "warm mount" of the incoming scene's logic 50ms before the scene actually opens, intended to give `afterMount` listeners a head start on data fetching. The cancellation path (when the user navigates away within those 50ms) calls `unmount()`, but the **success path drops the local `unmount` reference** without ever calling it. Net effect:

- The scene's logic counter never return

## Problem

The checkpoint lag metric used a sentinel value of `-1` to signal that the ingestion checkpoint was unavailable. This is a poor metric design - a gauge with a magic negative value requires special-casing in every dashboard and alert rule, and conflates two distinct signals (real lag vs. unavailability) into a single metric.

## Changes

Replaced the `-1` sentinel pattern with a dedicated counter metric:

- `record_checkpoint_lag` now only accepts a non-`None` checkpoint and records r

title: fix(visual-review): raise pixel diff threshold to reduce noise during beta
body:
## Problem

During beta, the 1% pixel diff threshold flags too many false positives from font anti-aliasing, sub-pixel rendering, and scrollbar jitter — especially on small components. Data shows 33% of all CHANGED snapshots are in the 1-2% band, and every human-tolerated diff maxed out at 2.3%.

## Changes

Raise `PIXEL_DIFF_THRESHOLD_PERCENT` from 1.0 to 2.5. SSIM safety net stays at 1% dissimilarity — it still catches real structural changes on tall pages where pixel ratio gets diluted.

Multiple PostHog folks have reported hitting `/admin` (no trailing slash) and getting silently redirected back to the web app instead of the Django admin — the classic "why can't I log in to admin?" moment that costs people a few minutes until someone remembers to add the slash.

On 2026-04-23 at 11:55 UTC, feature-flags `/flags` p99 stepped from ~94 ms to ~200 ms and Redis timeout errors jumped from a few per second into the thousands. Posthog-solo's slowlog was pinned at 128/128, with >99% of entries being `ZRANGE INSIGHT_CACHE_UPDATE_RECENTLY_ACCESSED_TEAMS 0 -1 WITHSCORES` at ~60 ms per call. The rate of these calls stepped from ~0.1/s to 2–8/s, burning roughly 22% of posthog-solo's single-threaded command loop and blocking every other client on that in

title: fix(popover): replace CSSTransition with a state-machine to stop DOM retention

## Problem

Legacy `Popover` used `react-transition-group`'s `CSSTransition` with `nodeRef` + `mountOnEnter` + `unmountOnExit` to coordinate a 50 ms fade + rotate animation on open/close. The library is unmaintained (last code change Sept 2022, no React 19 support for `findDOMNode` removal) and its class-component state machine races with React 18 concurrent rendering — we observed DOM retention as `Popover--exit-done` subtrees held past React unmount, the same failure mode we just fixed in `Lem

We want to remove the dependency. This PR knocks out the two small remaining usages.

**`LemonBadge`**

## Problem

Follow-up to #56235. `LemonTableLoader` wrapped its element in `react-transition-group`'s `CSSTransition` with `mountOnEnter` + `unmountOnExit` + `nodeRef`. Each dashboard refresh therefore leaked one loader subtree per insight tile. Called from four places (`InsightMeta`, `LemonTable`, `P

`MCPClientProfile` was being constructed *before* the flag-resolution

held at that earlier point, not the value reflecting the request props
used by the rest of `init()`.

Many PostHog devs use the [pi coding agent](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono/tree/main/packages/coding-agent) alongside or instead of Claude Code. title: fix(oauth-proxy): hash KV keys to survive long OAuth state values
body:
## Problem

`GET /oauth/authorize/` on `oauth.posthog.com` returns
`{"error":"server_error","error_description":"An internal error occurred"}`
when the OAuth `state` value is long enough that the KV key the proxy writes
exceeds Cloudflare Workers KV's **512-byte key-name limit**
([docs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/kv/platform/limits/)).

`services/oauth-proxy/src/handlers/authorize.ts` uses `state` verbatim as KV
key material (`region:<state>`, `callback:<state>`). OAuth 2.0 treats `state`

The loop only terminated when the agent stumbled onto the right name.

## Changes

1. **Structured errors on `/query/` failures** — HogQL errors now carry a `HogQLMet

Confluent's AIOProducer requires a loop when initializing, which it

Confluent's AIOProducer does not support a headers keyword argument to
produce: It will raise a RuntimeError just if the

title: chore(tasks): stop CI follow-up loop when no PR exists
body:
## Problem

When a task has a repository but the agent never creates a PR (e.g. research/analysis tasks), the CI follow-up loop polls `get_pr_context` indefinitely every 15 minutes. Each poll finds no PR URL in the task run output and skips the follow-up message, but never increments `_ci_repetitions`, so the loop never terminates. The workflow stays alive with an extended 16-min inactivity timeout for the entire duration, burning sandbox resources.

## Changes

- Change `_should_run_ci_follow_u

title: fix(devex): enable prometheus multiprocess mode for celery prefork workers
body:
**Problem**

Celery prefork workers each call `start_http_server(8001)` on init, but only one child successfully binds the port. Celery's `Signal.send()` silently catches the `OSError` for the rest. Prometheus scrapes that one child and sees ~1/N of actual task events — a 10-25x undercount on all `posthog_celery_task_*` metrics in Grafana.

Cross-referenced against phlower (which taps the Celery event stream directly and sees every event), the numbers confirm the gap scales with worker concu

title: refactor(redis): widen HINCRBY count type to i64
body:
## Problem

The `common-redis` wrapper capped HINCRBY counts at `i32` (~2.1B), but Redis natively supports the full `i64` range (64-bit signed integer). The cap existed only because the wrapper's single original caller incremented by 1 per request — any future caller that batches larger accumulated counts would need to saturate or narrow on every call.

## Changes

- `PipelineCommand::HIncrBy.count`: `i32` → `i64`; `Pipeline::hincrby()` builder param widened to match.
- `Client::hincrby`

title: feat(cdp): make kafka consumer rebalanceTimeoutMs configurable

## Problem

The Kafka consumer in the plugin-server has a hardcoded 20-second `rebalanceTimeoutMs` at `nodejs/src/kafka/consumer.ts:181`. When `CONSUMER_WAIT_FOR_BACKGROUND_TASKS_ON_REBALANCE=true` (which is on for all CDP services), the consumer pauses during a partition rebalance and waits up to this timeout for in-flight background tasks (Cyclotron writes, etc.) to drain. ## TL;DR

Add timezone tracking to scheduled changes so cron expressions resolve wall-clock fields (like "9am") in the project's timezone rather than UTC, preventing schedule drift across daylight saving time transitions.

## Problem

Cron expressions with wall-clock fields (e.g., "0 9 * * 1-5" for 9am weekdays) were being interpreted in UTC rather than the project's local timezone. This caused schedules to drift by an hour during DST transitions, as the UTC offset changed but the cron evaluatio

Long full-refresh Convex imports have no way to survive a worker restart — if a sync is interrupted mid-way through a large table, it starts the endpoint over. The Sentry data-warehouse source was a `SimpleSource`, so a full-refresh run that was interrupted (e.g. a worker restart mid-sync) had to start the endpoint over from the first page. title: fix(trends): prevent series cohort filters leaking into breakdown
body:
## Problem

If a trends chart breaks down by cohorts and a series filters by "person in cohort X", the filter cohort X shows up as an extra bar - even though it was never in the breakdown list.

```
Example
Create trends with two series:
- Series A ...
- Series B: filter by "person in cohort X"
Breakdown by cohorts: [Y, Z]

Expected: 2 bars (Y, Z)
Actual: 3 bars (X, Y, Z) — X leaks in
```

When the breakdown has 2+ cohorts, trends uses a single join that lumps every cohort mention

title: chore(data-warehouse): document why google sheets source stays non-resumable

## Problem

`GoogleSheetsSource` was evaluated for conversion from `SimpleSource` to `ResumableSource` (so long full-refresh runs could survive worker restarts). It doesn't fit: `gspread.Worksheet.get_all_records()` is a single Sheets API call that returns every row at once, with no pagination cursor, continuation token, or range handle exposed. The fetch loop yields exactly one batch — there's no intermediate checkpoint where some rows have been emitted and others are still pending.

This leave

title: ci: lint dorny/paths-filter negation safety
body:
## Problem

With dorny's default `predicate-quantifier: some`, each rule in a `filters:` block is OR'd independently. A `!path` rule then matches **every file NOT at that path** — including unrelated files — so the filter silently triggers on every PR.

This has slipped in twice now:

- #54739 — caught in review before merge
- #55920 — slipped through and shipped, fixed after the fact

Adds a repo-level lint so reviewers don't have to remember this foot-gun.

- New `.git

Data warehouse sources only had a single boolean `betaSource` flag on their `SourceConfig`, which rendered a "Beta" tag in the New source picker. title: fix(cdp): add logging and increase rate limits for hogflow invocations
body:
### Problem                                                                                                                     

Hogflow (workflow) invocations were being silently dropped when rate limited — no log entry visible in the workflow's activity log and no server-side warning. Customers experienced unexplained missing workflow executions with no way to diagnose the issue.

title: fix: support email dkim
body:
## Problem

Replies use Django's SMTP backend authed as PostHog's shared Mailgun user, so Mailgun DKIM-signs with d=mg.posthog.com. From: is the customer's domain, so DKIM doesn't align and the envelope return-path doesn't align for SPF either. DMARC fails, recipients reject with 5.7.26.

## Changes

Route outbound through POST https://api.mailgun.net/v3/<domain>/messages.mime, authed with the master Mailgun API key already in instance settings. Mailgun then signs with that domain's DKIM k

title: fix(endpoints): CTE variable propagation with scalar subqueries
body:
## Problem

When a HogQL endpoint with variables uses a scalar subquery that reads from another CTE in the same query (e.g. `WHERE timestamp = (SELECT max(ts) FROM other_cte)`), the variable-materialization analyzer was accepting it. Materialization went through, and only at read time did ClickHouse raise `Scalar subquery returned more than one row` — because once we propagate the variable column, the inner subquery now returns a row per variable value instead of one.

A second, smaller bug live

title: fix(llma): require write scope for skill file delete
body:
## Problem

\`@get_file.mapping.delete\` lets DELETE requests on the skill-file URL through with only the \`llm_skill:read\` scope. The bypass was flagged by the hex-security-app review bot on #55897 (incorrectly attached due to a squash-merge artifact, but the underlying claim was substantive).

Mechanism:

1. \`get_file\` is decorated with \`@action(required_scopes=["llm_skill:read"], …)\`.
2. DRF's router stores the action's kwargs (including \`required_scopes\`) as URL-pattern \`initkwargs\`

title: fix(dashboard): preserve desktop reading order on mobile
body:
## Problem

Dashboards with a clean 2-column desktop layout (e.g. `A B / C D / E F`) can display tiles in a seemingly random order on mobile (e.g. `A, E, F, B, ...`). Users can't fix this manually either, because layout editing is disabled on screens ≤ 768px.

The cause is in `calculateLayouts` (frontend/src/scenes/dashboard/tileLayouts.ts). The reference order used for the `xs` (mobile, 1-col) layout is seeded from the stored `sm` (y, x) coordinates via `sortTilesByLayout`. Tiles without a

But `process_events` applied those restrictions to every event in the batch regardless of `data_type`, which means analytics-scoped restrictions were leaking across pipelines:

- Exception events (`$exception`) are routed by the sink to a separate `error_tracking_topic` and consumed by the

While investigating detached-element telemetry (`detached_elements` event) we traced a portion of the `InsightTooltipWrapper-*` portal-container leak to a stale-id race in `useInsightTooltip`.

`tooltipId` was derived via `useMemo(() => Math.random(), [])`. `useMemo` is **not guaranteed** to preserve its value across renders — React can discard memoized values. When the id regenerates between:

Those keys are identical across teams, so the same storage slot

title: chore(experiments): use quantileExact in winsorization calculation
body:
## Problem

`quantile()` in ClickHouse uses a reservoir-sampling approximation, which produces slightly different winsorization bounds across runs of the same experiment query. For experiment results, where reproducibility of bounds matters the non-determinism is more costly than the extra compute.

## Changes

Switch the three winsorization bound expressions in `ExperimentQueryBuilder` from `quantile()` to `quantileExact()` so lower/upper percentile bounds are deterministic.

title: fix(scim): normalize member ids before set diff in role membership update
body:
Some IdPs send `members[].value` as a JSON number rather than a string. The set diff in `_update_members` was comparing those raw values against the always-string `current_user_ids`, so an integer-typed value the IdP asked to keep would land in `to_remove` and the membership would be silently deleted on PUT/replace.

Normalize incoming values to `str(int(...))` upfront, mirroring the current_user_ids form. Non-coercible values (e.g. nested-group UUIDs from Entra ID) are skipped + logged once a

title: fix(hogli): trigger shell mode for commands with variable refs
body:
## Problem

`DirectCommand.execute()` only uses `shell=True` when the command string contains operators (`&&`, `||`, `|`, `\n`). Commands that use `$@` or `$1` without those operators get run as a list — no shell expansion, so `$@` is passed as a literal string. `format:yaml` is affected today.

## Changes

Add `$` to the operator detection list so any command referencing shell variables gets routed through `shell=True`.

## How did you test this code?

Unit test added covering the fix. Existing

## Changes

Pilot git-based `dorny/paths-filter` change detection for six high-signal workflows:

title: fix(personhog): prewarm pg connections before signaling ready
body:
## Problem

During rolling deployments of personhog-replica, new pods start with completely cold DB connection pools.
The pool uses `connect_lazy()`, so zero connections exist at startup — even with `MIN_PG_CONNECTIONS` set, sqlx's background maintenance task only begins filling the pool after the first successful connection.
The readiness probe (`/_readiness`) only checks the shutdown token, not DB connectivity, so K8s routes traffic immediately while the pool is empty.
The first burst of

title: fix(cli): resolve release once in process command to avoid race condition
body:
## Problem

When running `posthog-cli sourcemap process`, the `inject` and `upload` steps each independently call `get_release_for_maps` → `fetch_or_create`. This means the release is created during `inject` (POST), then `upload` tries to look it up again (GET by hash). If the backend uses a read replica with replication lag, the second lookup can miss the just-created release and either fail or create a duplicate.

## Changes

- `inject_impl` and `upload` now accept an optional pre-resolv

title: fix(warehouse-sources): decode BSON UUIDs and out-of-range dates in MongoDB source
body:
## Problem

Two customer-reported MongoDB source issues, both rooted in the source's use of PyMongo's default codec options:

1. **UUID primary keys are unusable for SQL joins.** BSON Binary subtype 4 (UUID) values come through as `bson.Binary` objects whose `repr()` is a Python bytes literal like `b'\x00\x00\x00\x15\xaf\x12...'`. That string is ambiguous (printable bytes render as ASCII, non-printable as `\xNN`) so downstream SQL can't reliably decode it. One customer fell back to joining on em

title: feat(data_warehouse): adaptive window chunking for partitioned postgres
body:
## Problem

Large partitioned Postgres parents (500M+ rows, 1TB+) timeout on initial sync. A single server-side cursor with `ORDER BY created_at` on a partitioned parent triggers an `Append + Sort` across every child relation — the planner materializes the full sort before the first row returns, blowing `statement_timeout`. Customers hit this when the incremental field's index isn't attached to every child.

## Changes

- New module `posthog/temporal/data_imports/sources/postgres/partitioned_tab

Zap failures are silent — customers get nothing, we find out only when someone complains — and the handoff through `webhook_secret` is an ad-hoc auth story that only exists because we didn't have a stable identifier we both agreed on. title: fix(worker): let celery drain in-flight tasks on deploy
body:
## Problem

Every deploy kills in-flight Celery tasks because the entrypoint script (`bin/docker-worker-celery`) doesn't wait for celery's warm shutdown. Bash (PID 1) sends SIGTERM to the celery worker but exits immediately — the container tears down before tasks can drain, wasting the 1230s `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` entirely.

This causes ClickHouse "Connection reset by peer" errors for queries that were in flight when the pod was terminated.

A second issue: `process_run_diffs` (visual r

title: fix(dags): retry sessions backfill on TOO_MANY_PARTS
body:
## Problem

The experimental sessions v3 backfill occasionally hits `TOO_MANY_PARTS` even though the preflight `wait_for_parts_to_merge` check succeeded immediately beforehand.

After reviewing, the preflight query itself looks correct:

title: fix(logs-alerts): give BROKEN and ERRORED notifications pre-commit retry semantics
body:
## Problem

Notification delivery for alert state transitions (errored and broken) was best-effort and fire-and-forget — if the notification failed, the state was already committed and there was no retry path. This meant missed notifications were silently accepted with no recovery mechanism.

## Changes

- Added a `BROKEN` value to `NotificationAction` so that transitioning to the broken state is treated as a first-class notification action, consistent with how `ERROR` and other transitions are

title: feat: Allow partial person splitting
body: ## Problem The existing split person endpoint is all-or-nothing — it splits every distinct ID off of a person. Support has had cases where many profiles were accidentally merged into one mega person and only some of those IDs need to be extracted, leaving the rest merged and the original person's properties intact. Today that requires either splitting the whole person and rebuilding, or editing data out of band.

title: feat(tasks): skip CI follow-up reruns when PR is closed or unchanged

## Problem

The `ProcessTaskWorkflow` fires a CI follow-up on every CI event for the task's PR. That causes spurious reruns when the PR hasn't actually changed (e.g. duplicate webhook deliveries, unrelated check-runs) and pointless work when the PR has already been closed. Each spurious follow-up wakes the sandbox and consumes the agent's CI retry budget.

## Changes

- Added `GitHubIntegration.get_pull_request` / `get_pull_request_from_url` / `parse_pull_request_url` helpers on `posthog/models/

title: fix(modeling): try to fix starved queue in modeling temporal
body:
## Problem

15 min schedules get way behind bc of failures and then sync all at once. here's my theory:

1. schedules run as normal
2. an influx of schedule runs causes the queue to run behind
3. new jobs start and attempt to preempt old jobs but they can't because they are on the same queue that is already lagging behind
4. the problem gets worse and worse until new jobs stop being queued long enough to allow temporal to catch up

title: feat(personhog): router client is multi channel
body:
## Problem

The personhog-router maintains a single gRPC channel (HTTP/2 connection) to the personhog-replica K8s service. Because K8s service load balancing is L4 (TCP-level), that single long-lived connection pins all traffic to one replica pod. With 3 replica pods and 3 router pods, each deployment is a roll of the dice, where one pod can end up handling the majority of requests while others sit idle.

## Changes

**Multi-channel support (personhog-router):**
- `ReplicaBackend` now ope

## Problem

The Rust workspace declared `tokio = "1.34.0"` as the minimum version. Cargo's lockfile happened to resolve to 1.50.0, but the declared minimum did not enforce the soundness fix introduced in tokio 1.42.1 — that release fixed an unsoundness in the broadcast channel where `clone()` could be called on `Send + !Sync` values without proper synchronization. Raising the declared minimum guarantees the fix is in place across all consumers and prevents anyone from ever resolving back below i

- **Assignment rules** — so agents can explain _why_ an issue was auto-assigned (or wasn't).

The tracking code (`ph_id`) dropped `parentRunId` at send time, so the webhook couldn't recover it.

A Visual Review tab sitting in the background was observed growing from ~200MB to ~400MB of browser-reported footprint. Two V8 heap snapshots taken before and after that growth showed **the JS heap was stable** (143MB -> 142MB, zero new detached DOM nodes).

That means the 200MB of growth is happening outside V8 — in decoded image memory, compositor/paint layers, and renderer-internal buffers that heap snapshots don't capture.

`VisualReviewRunScene.tsx` renders a thum

title: fix(dev): honor CADDY_HOST in docker-compose.dev

## Problem

Follow-up to #54995. That PR added the `trusted_proxies` global block to `docker-compose.dev.yml` but left the site address hardcoded as `http://localhost:8000`. Because `dev.yml` overrides `environment.CADDYFILE` wholesale, the `${CADDY_HOST:-...}` substitution already present in `base.yml` is bypassed — so callers running dev behind a proxy (ngrok, Tailscale Funnel, Coder workspaces, etc.) that set `CADDY_HOST=:8000` were silently ignored, and non-localhost Host headers fell throug

## Problem

The MCP `cdp-functions-logs-retrieve` tool (and the logs endpoints more broadly) accept a `search` query param meant to do a case-insensitive substring match on log messages. In practice, the parent viewset's `SearchFilter` was intercepting the `search` param first and using it to filter by function name / other searchable fields on the parent queryset, so the ClickHouse log-message search never saw it.

Noticed while wiring up the MCP tool in #55154 - splitting the one-line fix out

It would be interesting to see if this could work well for experiment queries also.

After removing DLT from the rest_source (#54307), the custom `Resource.__iter__` yields **pages** (`list[dict]`), not individual dicts. TikTok and Snapchat Ads handled this correctly for report/stats endpoints (via `process_resources` which flattens pages), but the entity/account branch did `items = dlt_resources[0]`, leaving a raw `Resource`.

When downstream transforms iterated `items`, each element was a page (a list). Calls like `report.copy()` then produced a list copy, and `rep

title: refactor(data-warehouse): make Linear source resumable
body:
## Problem

Full-refresh syncs of the Linear data-warehouse source restart from page 1 when a worker is restarted mid-sync. For workspaces with many issues/comments, this means re-fetching hundreds of pages and can prevent the sync from ever finishing within its time budget.

## Changes

Convert `LinearSource` from `SimpleSource` to `ResumableSource`:

- Add `LinearResumeConfig(cursor: str)` dataclass holding the GraphQL `endCursor` to resume from.
- Thread `ResumableSourceManager[LinearResumeCo

title: feat(tasks): drop sandbox inactivity timeout to 5 minutes
body:
## Problem

The ProcessTaskWorkflow inactivity timeout was 30 minutes, which keeps sandboxes (and their OAuth tokens) alive far longer than needed. While a user is babysitting a run, heartbeats continually reset the timer, so the sandbox outlives the 6h token TTL and requests start failing. It also costs compute.

The prior concern — flaky SSE reconnect handling — is less of an issue now that heartbeats are reliable. Shrinking the timeout also forces us to keep reconnect paths honest.

## Change

`RedditAdsSource` was a `SimpleSource`, so a worker restart mid-sync forced the
endpoint to restart from scratch. Other sources (klaviyo, hubspot, temporalio,
stripe) already persist a per-job cursor to the data-warehouse Redis and
resume from the last yielded page on restart.

Since PR #54307 replaced the dlt-based REST framework with a PostHog-owned one
at `posthog/temporal/data_imports/sources/common/rest_source/`, no source had
yet combined `ResumableSource` with that framework —

The data warehouse API serializers declare several fields as read-only that the view methods actually accept from `request.data`, bypassing serializer validation. This means:

This is the follow-up from #55393 (DWH MCP tools PR).

**ExternalDataSchemaSerializer** (partial-update surface):

The GitHub data-warehouse source uses the shared `rest_api_resources` dlt
framework, which keeps pagination state inside an in-memory `BasePaginator`
subclass. On a worker restart mid-sync, that state is lost — long
full-refresh runs have to start the endpoint over from page one every time.

## Changes

- `github.py` — replaces the `rest_api_resource(...)` wiring (and its
  associated `GithubPaginator`) with a hand-written paginated fetch loop
  modelled on the klaviyo source. The ne

Users opening session replays are hitting growing 408 timeouts on the comments sidebar — 183 exceptions across 182 unique users in the last 30 days, with weekly counts climbing from ~16 (week of Mar 23) to a 75 peak (week of Apr 13).

Every replay open triggers `loadRecordingComments` in `sessionRecordingCommentsLogic.ts`, which issues `GET /api/projects/:id/comments?item_id=<recordingId>` *without* a `scope` param. The backend filter in `posthog/api/comments.py` can then only narrow

`BuildBetterSource` currently extends `SimpleSource`, so any full-refresh that gets interrupted (worker restart, retry, etc.) has to re-fetch every page of every endpoint from offset 0. For large BuildBetter workspaces that means duplicated work and, in pathological cases, no forward progress.

title: chore(data-warehouse): document why DoIt source is not resumable
body:
## Problem

The DoIt data-warehouse source was evaluated for conversion from `SimpleSource` to `ResumableSource` so that long full-refresh runs could resume after a worker restart. On inspection, the source does not have a pagination loop — `doit_source` issues a single `GET /analytics/v1/reports/{report_id}` and yields one batch per run. Converting it to `ResumableSource` would only persist state that duplicates the existing `db_incremental_field_last_value` → `startDate` behavior, adding no re

title: fix(growth): reduce memory use in sync colleagues/cross-sell ops
body:
These Dagster ops were OOMKilled in production while processing batches of
1000 teams. Each team iterated `all_users_with_access()` with the default
chunk size and hydrated full `User` rows (including four JSONFields), then
skipped users in Python — allocator pressure compounded over ~1000 teams
per subprocess and peaked at 10-way concurrency.

Shrink per-row footprint with `.only("id", "allow_sidebar_suggestions")`,
filter skipped users at the DB level, pin `chunk_size=500`, and
`close_old_conn

title: feat: funnel binary
body:
## Problem
We're using JSON to talk to the UDF.

Ran a benchmark of the options:
RowBinary gets a 10% speedup over JSON.
MsgPack gets about a 3% speedup.
WASM is slower.

## Changes
Switched to RowBinary to get the 10% speedup.

## How did you test this code?
Passes posthog tests. Tested locally. Ran queries. 

## Publish to changelog?
No

"Shared metric" and several other entries appear out of alphabetical order in the Personal API Keys scope picker (Settings → User → Personal API keys) and the CLI authorize flow.

title: fix(llma): allow personal API keys on evaluation report runs/generate

## Problem

The nested `runs` and `generate` endpoints on `EvaluationReportViewSet` rejected personal API key requests with `This action does not support Personal API Key access`. Hit while dogfooding evaluation reports through the PostHog MCP — `evaluation-report-get` worked, but `evaluation-report-runs-list` returned 403.

Root cause: DRF's default scope resolver in `APIScopePermission` only maps standard CRUD action names (`list`, `retrieve`, `create`, etc.) onto `<scope_object>:<read|write>`

## Changes

- Inlined all 3 rule modal logics as standard `kea()` calls so kea-typegen can auto-generate their types

title: fix(taxonomy): collapse seen_together N+1 into a single query

## Problem

The `GET /api/projects/<id>/property_definitions/seen_together` endpoint was issuing one `EXISTS` SQL query **per event name** against `posthog_eventproperty`, rather than a single aggregate query. On projects with a large event-property table and multi-event session-recording playlist filters, the cumulative latency exceeded the gateway timeout and the endpoint returned HTTP 408.

User impact is bounded but real: when this endpoint times out, the frontend caller `loadEventsHaveSessi

title: fix(taxonomic-filter): guard appendTopMatches against missing group

Stack runs `loadRemoteItemsSuccess` → `infiniteListResultsReceived` → `appendTopMatches`, mapping uniquely to `items[0].group` in `taxonomicFilterLogic.tsx`.

The reducer guards `items.length === 0` but not against an array whose first element is undefined/null or lacks `.group`. T

title: perf(experiments): use grace_hash join for mean metric queries
body:
## Problem

Some experiment mean metric queries are erroring out due to high memory usage. The default ClickHouse hash join holds the right-hand side fully in memory, which can blow past memory limits when joining the exposures table with a large metric-events table.

## Changes

In `_evaluate_experiment_query`, conditionally set `join_algorithm='grace_hash'` on the `HogQLGlobalSettings` when the metric is an `ExperimentMeanMetric`. `grace_hash` partitions the build side and spills to disk

title: feat(logs): anchor alert windows on ingestion checkpoint
body:
## Problem

Alert windows for logs alerting were anchored to wall-clock `now`, which means they could query ahead of what ClickHouse has actually ingested. On both the dev `MergeTree` shard and the prod `AggregatingMergeTree` between merges, a bare `min(max_observed_timestamp)` scans every raw insert and returns the oldest value ever written. This caused alert windows to potentially miss recent log data or include gaps where ingestion hadn't caught up yet.

Additionally, quiet partitions (those

title: fix(data-warehouse): handle 408 timeout on external data source jobs polling
body:
## Problem

The Source settings page polls `GET /external_data_sources/:id/jobs/` every 5s. For sources with many historical jobs, the combination of `prefetch_related('schema')` plus DRF serialization occasionally exceeds the edge request budget and returns a 408. Error tracking issue `019da153-4e6b-7b73-b80c-574d59561910` (first seen 2026-04-18) captures the resulting frontend exception from `lib/api.ts`; each 408 reschedules the poll, turning transient slowness into repeated noise.

## Change

title: fix(llma): include reasoning tokens in cost for gemini 3 models
body:
## Problem

Events for Gemini 3.x models report `$ai_reasoning_tokens` separately from `$ai_output_tokens` (unlike OpenAI o-series and Anthropic thinking, where reasoning tokens are already inside output tokens). The server-side cost calculator was only adding reasoning tokens to the output cost for Gemini 2.5, so Gemini 3 reports came out undercounted by the reasoning-token multiplier.

Reported in https://github.com/PostHog/posthog-js/issues/3160 with a `gemini-3-flash-preview` event that had

The root cause: `retrieve` bypassed the facade layer and queried the ORM directly, passing raw Django model objects to `ErrorTrackingExternalReferenceSerializer`. The serializer's `get_external_url` method expects facade contract types that have a pre-computed `external_url` field, but

`processLogMessageBuffer` only exposed coarse tracing: Avro decode/encode had no spans, and JSON work used a single `jsonParseLogRecords` span that mixed `parseLogBodyForIngestion` with `enrichLogRecordWithJsonAttributes`. PII-only mode ran parse inside the scrub span, so scrub time included parse.

## Changes

title: fix(phai): mcp string results no longer char-expand, drop truncation
body:
## Problem

MCP tool results with a plain-string handler return (`execute-sql`, `read-data-schema`, `read-data-warehouse-schema`) were arriving at the model as a YAML-like dump with one character per line:

```
"0": "{"
"1": "\""
"2": n
"3": a
...
```

Root cause: `mcp.ts` destructured the handler result with `{ ...rawResult } = await handler(params)`. On a primitive string, JS coerces to a `String` wrapper and the rest pattern collects its enumerable indexed properties, yielding `{"0":"{", "1":

The Elixir SDK snippet shown during the in-app onboarding flow at `/insights/quick-start` has two issues that block customers from getting started:

1. The version specifier `~> 2.2.0` resolves to `>= 2.2.0 and < 2.3.0` in Mix, so installs are pinned to the `2.2.x` patch series. The latest release on Hex.pm is `2.6.0`, but customers following the snippet end up on `2.2.0`.

title: chore(devex): clarify mypy guidance in AGENTS.md

**Problem**

The mypy guidance was split across two places and vague — "mypy-strict style" in Code Style plus "do not run mypy" in Lint. Agents didn't know what "strict style" actually means in practice.

**Changes**

Consolidate into one Code Style bullet with actionable rules: annotate all signatures, avoid `Any`, use `TYPE_CHECKING`. Note that mypy is too slow to run locally but CI runs it on every PR.

title: feat(cdp): add calendar-day deduplication options for destinations

Adds two new trigger options for destinations: "Run once per person per calendar day" and "Run once per person per event name per calendar day". These use the date in the masking hash (via formatDateTime(now())) so the dedup boundary is the calendar day, not a rolling time window.

This solves the case where a 24h rolling interval suppresses events across day boundaries (e.g. 3pm Monday event masks 10am Tuesday event).

closes https://posthog.com/questions/custom-deduplication-logic

title: fix: updated log default retention to a valid retention day (14,30,90)
body:

Logs ingestion defaulted `retention-days` to a value that did not match what teams can store via the API (`logs_settings.retention_days`). That made it easy for ingestion to emit headers inconsistent with settings users can PATCH.

title: fix(exports): truncate error envelope to fit Temporal's 2 MiB payload
body:
## Problem

Export activity failures raise `ApplicationError(str(e), error_trace, ...)`. When the upstream exception has a multi-MB `str()` — a ClickHouse 5xx with a verbose error body or a Playwright timeout embedding rendered HTML — the envelope exceeds Temporal's 2 MiB gRPC payload limit. Temporal rejects the activity with `PayloadSizeError` (`TMPRL1103`), the workflow burns its retry budget, and the subscription delivery times out. ~30 failures / ~10 teams over 72h in prod.

## Changes

Adds

title: feat(insights): hint to alias SQL breakdown expressions
body:
## Problem

On an insight breakdown, if a user picks the SQL expression option and enters a non-trivial expression (e.g. `countIf(properties.$browser = 'Chrome')`), the raw expression is used verbatim as the column/series label. That's unreadable in charts and tables. Today, a user can work around this by aliasing the expression — either with `AS column_name` or with a trailing `-- column_name` comment — but there's no in-product hint that this is an option.

title: chore(llm-gateway): remove unsupported gemini provider
body:
## Problem

The LLM gateway had `gemini` / `vertex_ai` wired into the provider registry (API key setting, env-var passthrough, and model filtering), but the runtime doesn't install `litellm[google]`. When a request came in for a gemini model, litellm raised an error saying the `litellm[google]` extra was required, which was causing pod-level errors.

Since we don't actually support gemini end-to-end yet, the simplest fix is to drop the partial wiring rather than pull in the extra for a provider

title: fix(alerts): prefetch related rows in alert list queryset
body:
## Problem

Loading a single page of alerts in the UI (`ALERTS_PER_PAGE = 30`) fans out into ~90+ extra DB queries. `AlertSerializer.to_representation` accesses three relations that aren't on the list queryset:

- `subscribed_users.all()` — M2M with no `prefetch_related`, one query per row
- `threshold` — FK not in `select_related`, one query per row that has a threshold
- `created_by` — FK not in `select_related`, one query per row

The base queryset only `select_related`d `team` and `insight`.

title: fix(personhog): clamp person batch size at server limit of 250

The personhog-replica service enforces a `MAX_BATCH_LOOKUP_SIZE` of 250 IDs per request for person record lookups (`GetPersonsByUuids`, `GetPersons`, `GetPersonsByDistinctIdsInTeam`). The `PersonStrategy` in `actor_strategies.py` was sending batches of 1000 IDs directly to the gRPC client, bypassing the batched helpers in `util.py` that already respected this limit. This caused the replica to reject requests with `InvalidArgument`, triggering fallback to raw SQL.

The retry shim only drops `is not present`, so it hard-fails — leaving raw `coder start` as the only workaround.

title: fix(data-warehouse): only require prefix when a no-prefix source exists
body:
## Problem

Closes #60559

When a team has at least one non-deleted data warehouse source of a given type, the create flow required a prefix on every new source of that type, even if every existing source already had a distinct prefix. The check is broader than the table-name collision constraint it appears to be protecting: a no-prefix source plus any number of prefixed sources of the same type have separate table namespaces (`build_table_name` uses `{prefix or ''}{source_type}_{schema_name

title: fix(sources): postgres multi-schema doesn't toggle should_sync on discovery
body:
## Problem

Managed Postgres warehouse sources could have their table syncs disabled repeatedly and without any error in the sync logs. The per-source schema-discovery pass reconciled stored schema rows against freshly-discovered table names by exact string match. Since Postgres discovery emits qualified (schema.table) or bare (table) names depending on whether a single schema is configured, a source whose stored rows used a different qualification than current discovery had every live row treat

title: feat(llm-gateway): emit $ai_input_cost_usd and $ai_output_cost_usd
body:
## Problem

The llm-gateway captures `$ai_generation` events with only `$ai_total_cost_usd` set (sourced from LiteLLM's `response_cost`). Ingestion's cost calculator in `nodejs/src/ingestion/ai/costs/index.ts::processCost` short-circuits to the passthrough path only when **both** `$ai_input_cost_usd` and `$ai_output_cost_usd` are present on the event. Since the gateway sets neither, ingestion falls into the model-lookup path and rederives the per-side costs from its bundled OpenRouter catalog.

## Changes

- Renderers in `slack_thread.py` accept `task_url: str | None`. When `None`, the PostHog Code button is dropped (PR-bearing messages keep "View PR").

title: fix(ws): stop v3 sync jobs getting stuck in running
body:
## Problem

V3 warehouse-source syncs sometimes finish with a split state: the data loads fine and the `ExternalDataSchema` is `Completed`, but the `ExternalDataJob` is stuck in `Running` with `finished_at = NULL` forever.
Nothing fixes it automatically, so it shows as a phantom "still running" sync and inflates the "running jobs" counts used for billing/usage.

It's a race between two processes that both write the job row:

- the **consumer** (`warehouse-sources-load`) marks the job `Com

title: fix(ci): unblock test-timing auto-commit and de-tax durations

## Problem

Django CI sharding runs on `.test_durations`, which is collected on master and reused to balance PR shards. Two things were degrading it:

- The update-test-timing workflow was failing at the commit step (`ghcommit-action` defaults `file_pattern` to `.` and choked on the leftover `junit_artifacts/` download dir — `read junit_artifacts/: is a directory`), so a fresh `.test_durations` never landed. The committed file was ~5 months stale.
- The migration-tax correction missed carriers.

title: fix(llm-gateway): refresh provider sets when model_cost reloads

LiteLLM's `get_llm_provider` resolves bare model names against module-level sets (`anthropic_models`, `open_ai_chat_completion_models`, etc.) populated only at import time. Refreshing `litellm.model_cost` doesn't touch them, so models added upstream after process start (e.g. `claude-opus-4-8` added 7h ago) raise `LLM Provider NOT provided` on the gateway until a redeploy.

## Changes

Call `litellm.add_known_models(model_cost)` after every `litellm.model_cost = …` reassignment in `Co

## Problem

Batch-triggered workflows (e.g. broadcasts) silently created new person profiles instead of updating existing ones when a step used a capture-based template like *Update person properties*.

Root cause: the batch trigger's blast-radius query returns person UUIDs from ClickHouse, so invocations were enqueued with an empty `event.distinct_id`. The `Update person properties` template defaults its input to `{event.distinct_id}`. When that resolved to empty, the `postHogCapture` runti

body:
## Problem

The experiment API serializer writes a `fingerprint` into `saved_metric.query` before returning it to the frontend (`presentation/serializers.py:332`). The frontend sends that metric — fingerprint and all — back to `/query`, where the fingerprint ends up in the cache key. The Temporal activity was reading the raw `saved_metric.query` from the DB (no fingerprint), so it warmed a different cache key than the one `/query` reads from. The previous fix only handled `breakdownFilter`; the

body:
## Problem

The LLM grounds its reasoning by inserting `(event_id <16-hex-hash>)` markers in prose; rendering them as deep-link chips needed a side-channel `event_id_mapping` (hash → uuid/timestamp) carried alongside the model output. Two downsides:

- Output consumers that don't render citations (Slack, downstream summaries, embeddings, customer analytics) had to either strip the parens themselves or ship raw `(event_id ...)` syntax. The reasoning is already emitted as `scanner_output_reasoning

title: feat(property-vals): cap top-K values per key in merger
body:
## Problem

The merger's produce volume is dominated by high-cardinality property keys. A handful of keys (ids, request hashes, timestamps in values, and similar) emit a near-unique value on almost every event, so each flush ships a long tail of one-off tuples that are useless for autocomplete: nobody benefits from a property value that was seen once and will never recur. That tail is the bulk of what we write to the output topic and, downstream, into ClickHouse.

Exclusion lists only help f

title: fix(phai): support SQL insights when adding to a dashboard
body:
## Problem

PostHog AI can create SQL-backed insights (a `DataVisualizationNode` whose source is a `HogQLQuery`) through its `execute_sql` tool, but it could not add them to a dashboard.
When a user asked PostHog AI to add a SQL insight to a dashboard, the `upsert_dashboard` tool failed with a pydantic validation error:

```
1 validation error for InsightVizNode
source
  Input tag 'DataVisualizationNode' found using 'kind' does not match any of the expected tags: 'TrendsQuery', 'FunnelsQ

title: chore(personhog): add latency decomposition metrics
body:
## Problem

When debugging personhog request latency in production, we can see a few high level latency metrics but things aren't isntrumented granularly enough to fully determine *where* time is being spent. is it the client delivering the request body? HTTP/2 channel backpressure? Network RTT? Replica DB queries? Gzip compression? As we add migrate more calls to the service, we want to easily be able to originate any latency problems.

## Changes

Adds fine-grained latency decomposition

title: fix(marketing-analytics): fix conversion goal precompute materialization
body:
## Problem

The conversion-goal precompute (lazy materialization of attributed conversions) produced **incorrect results**. The materialization INSERT was round-tripped through `to_printed_hogql` and then string-substituted/re-parsed, which introduced two bugs:

- **Timezone**: the per-job time window was injected as far-future sentinel datetimes, then swapped for `{time_window_min/max}` via a regex keyed on the UTC string. `to_printed_hogql` renders datetime constants in the *team's* timezone,

title: chore(ci): make foss/private sync atomic and faster
body:
The current `PostHog/git-sync` action has two issues:

- It's slow as hell, usually taking between 6 and 12 minutes, because it copies the entire 4GiB+ history every time
- It's not atomic. The `ee/` directory and our proprietary license are copied over first and then the removal/license change happens at some later point. The workflow could fail between the two steps, leaving posthog-foss with a non-MIT license.

This PR replaces `PostHog/git-sync` with a `blob:none` partial-clone checkout

title: feat(property-vals): add per-tuple count distribution metric at flush
body:
## Problem

We can see distinct-tuples-per-flush (`flush_tuples`) but not the distribution of per-tuple counts, so there's no real-time way to tell whether output volume is driven by unique-value cardinality (every tuple seen once) versus values that actually aggregate. That distinction is what decides whether the lever is dropping high-cardinality keys or something else.

A ClickHouse query on `property_values` confirms the volume is cardinality: over a recent window the median `property_count`

title: chore(ci): commit foss-sync changes via GitHub API
body: Replace the EndBug/add-and-commit step with planetscale/ghcommit-action so the posthog-foss sync commit is created through GitHub's GraphQL API. The enterprise/dependabot removals previously handled by the action's `remove` input are now explicit working-tree deletions, which ghcommit-action detects and applies.

## Summary

- Wrap `sccache --start-server` in a 3-attempt retry loop (5s delay between attempts) to handle transient WebDAV 500 errors that have been causing ~1-2 CI failures per week for 4+ months
- On persistent failure, gracefully degrade by unsetting `RUSTC_WRAPPER` so builds complete (slower, without cache) rather than failing the entire job

## Context

Related insight: [sccache WebDAV backend 500 errors causing recurring Rust CI

title: feat(property-vals): label worker metrics by worker
body:
## Problem

The events, groups, and merger workers all share one `worker_loop` and increment the same unlabeled metrics counters. So `property_vals_rs_events_received_total` conflated the all-teams event firehose (events worker) with the merger consuming the internal `property_vals_intermediate` topic. This made the throughput panels unreadable: raising the rollout pushed "received" up purely from extra merger consumption, while the real firehose stayed flat, and there was no way to tell the two

title: fix(oauth): translate pgbouncer query_wait_timeout into 503 on token refresh
body:
## Problem

A new error tracking issue (`KeyError: 'user'`) fired 114 times in a ~2.5 minute window from `OAuthTokenView.post`, affecting 114 distinct users trying to refresh OAuth tokens. The surface `KeyError` is a symptom — the root cause is PgBouncer killing the FK lookup on `OAuthRefreshToken.user` inside django-oauth-toolkit's `validate_refresh_token` with `query_wait_timeout` (transient connection-pool saturation). The resulting `django.db.OperationalError` propagated unhandled, becoming

title: chore(ci): add pytest-durations phase timing to django shards
body:
## Problem

We can already measure a Django CI shard's test-call time (JUnit) and its pre-pytest steps (GHA per-step timings), but nothing split the *in-pytest* time into fixture-setup vs test-call vs teardown, or named which fixtures are expensive. That split is what tells us where the shard floor actually is.

## Changes

Adds the maintained [`pytest-durations`](https://github.com/blake-r/pytest-durations) plugin (MIT) as a test dependency, enabled only on the Core and Temporal shard steps via

Brand-new users see a banner on every page when their project has no events: "This project has no events yet. title: feat(experiments): report precomputation failures to error tracking
body:
## Problem

When experiment exposure or metric-events precomputation fails, the query runner catches the exception, logs it, and falls back to a direct scan — so the query still returns correct results. That fallback is good for resilience, but it also **masks** the failure: a completely broken precomputation path looks like normal background retry noise while results keep flowing via the direct scan.

This is exactly how the `toUInt8` funnel-preaggregation bug (#60380) went unnoticed in product

title: chore(personhog): enforce 250-ID limit on batch person lookup RPCs
body:
## Problem

Personhog batch person lookup RPCs (`GetPersonsByDistinctIdsInTeam`, `GetPersonsByUuids`, `GetPersonsByDistinctIds`, `GetPersons`) accept unbounded ID lists, leading to large gRPC response payloads. These large responses increase memory pressure on the personhog-replica pods and risk hitting gRPC message size limits.

## Changes

**Server-side validation (Rust):**
- Added `MAX_BATCH_LOOKUP_SIZE = 250` constant in `personhog-replica`
- Added `Status::invalid_argument` validati

## Problem

The taxonomic-filter extraction stack (10 PRs, #59817 through #59826) split sub-logics out of `taxonomicFilterLogic.tsx` to shrink the file and make the picker easier to maintain. The structural shape of the refactor turned out to be wrong: every new sub-logic adds one input to the parent's `taxonomicGroups` selector, and kea's `SelectorTuple` type maxes out at 16 entries. We hit that limit repeatedly during the stack, kept patching it with one-shot "combine two adjacent selectors in

title: chore(django): reduce person fetch batch limit to 250
body:
## Problem

Person records can be large, so the tails of fetching persons by ids response sizes can end up ballooning. The tradeoff of more requests/lookups to have less data over the wire for a single response response appears to be the correct one.

## Changes

Reduce the batch limit on fetching person ids from 500 at a time to 250.

## How did you test this code?

Current tests already cover this. Just reducing the batch size number which will have to be tested on deploy.

## Changes

`EnvVarProvider::get_credentials` now resolves each credential across three sources, in order:

1. process environment
2. `./.env`
3. `./.env.local`

Process env wins; `.env` wins ov

## Goal

Give operators a clear answer to two questions about the lazy_computation framework:

1. **Are PG jobs flowing as fast as we're creating them?** — i.e. is the system keeping up.
2. **When we miss the cache, how much work does that translate to?** — i.e. how big are the recomputes we're paying for.

title: fix(mcp): restore connectionId on execute-sql tool
body:
## Problem

The `query-run` MCP tool accepted a `HogQLQuery` body, which carried an optional `connectionId` for routing queries against an external data warehouse connection (e.g. DuckLake, direct Postgres). The replacement `execute-sql` tool's input schema is just `{ query, truncate }` — there is no way to target a non-default connection, so users that relied on `connectionId` can no longer query their external warehouse via the MCP.

## Changes

- `ExecuteSQLMCPToolArgs` gains an optional `con

Reuses `getReferenceStep`, `getStepBreakdown

## Problem

The existing `CdpCyclotronV2WriteFailures` alert keys off `instrumented_function_duration_seconds_count{function="cyclotron_v2.bulk_create_jobs",success="false"}`. That bucket counts every failure path through `bulkCreateJobs` — including Zod input-validation failures (e.g. a customer sending a webhook trigger with `distinct_id` as a number instead of a string). Customer-shaped input then drives noise into a Workflows-team alert that should only fire for real system problems.

title: fix(logs): allow 0 as threshold count for log alerts
body:
## Problem

When configuring a logs alert to fire on at least 1 matching log, users have to
set `threshold_count = 0` with the `above` operator (count > 0). The serializer
rejected this with `min_value=1`, surfacing as **"Ensure this value is greater
than or equal to 1."** in the UI. There is no straightforward workaround —
`threshold_count = 1` requires at least 2 matching logs, which is not what the
user is asking for.

## Changes

- Drop `MinValueValidator(1)` from `LogsAlertConfiguration.thr

title: fix(data-warehouse): duckdb table functions denylist
body:
## Problem

DuckDB/DuckGres table functions like `read_csv`, `read_parquet`, `glob`, and `postgres_query` read local files or remote URLs. Exposing them to introspected `FROM func(args)` calls allows arbitrary file read and SSRF.

## Changes

- Add `is_dangerous_table_function()` — denylist of file/network readers (including `query`, which evaluates arbitrary SQL) plus `read_`/`scan_` prefix and `_scan`/`_attach` suffix heuristics.
- Filter them out of connection metadata at ingest, and reject t

## Changes

Indexer is removed. Summarizers gain an opt-in `emits_embeddings` flag in `scanner_config`. When set, the LLM is called against an extended response schema with `intent` / `outcome` / `friction_points` / `keywords`, the prompt asks for them, and the workflow dispatches the embedding side-effect activity. When off, the LLM sees only the lean titl

When a workflow / hog function input refers to an integration that no longer exists, `IntegrationChoice` silently substitutes the missing reference for the first available integration of the same kind. The UI then displays the substitute, but the persisted config still points at the dead ID until the user saves.

On `/settings/organization-details`, the org-level toggles — **Enable AI training on anonymized data**, **Discard client IP data by default for new projects**, and **Enable PostHog features that use third-party AI services** — are disabled for users below Admin level, but the tooltip explaining *why* used the generic `useRestrictedArea` message ("This area is restricted to organization admins and up. Your level is …"), which doesn't directly tell members what they can do about it.

title: chore(mcp-analytics): retire MCPSession backfill columns from ORM state
body:
## Problem

`MCPSession` is now just an on-demand **intent store** keyed by `(team, session_id)`. The session list is aggregated on the fly from `mcp_tool_call` events, and the Temporal backfill that once populated `session_start`, `session_end`, `duration_seconds`, `tools_used`, `tool_call_count`, `distinct_id`, and `mcp_client_name` was removed in #60039. Those seven columns are now dead weight — nothing writes them, and (as it turns out) the one place that still read one was silently broken.

  was valid; the email then failed at runtime with a raw Zod error in the logs.

  Two bugs combined:

  1. **Wrong field checked.** The editor validated `from.email`, but the runtime schema requires `from.integrationId`
  (and `from.email`).
  2. **Errors were swallowed.** Even when the email-specific validator did flag a problem, a generic validator ran
  af

title: feat(personhog): extract x-caller-tag header in router
body:
## Problem

Multiple services (Django, Node.js, Rust) consume the personhog gRPC API, but the only attribution today is `x-client-name`, which identifies the **service** (e.g., "posthog-django") but not **which code path within** that service made the call. Some code paths request too many persons, generating responses up to 65 MB that destabilize the service. Finding the offending callers is whack-a-mole without finer-grained attribution.

## Changes

Add a new `x-caller-tag` gRPC metadata head

title: feat(replay-vision): monthly observation quota

- `compute_quota_snapshot(org_id)` counts `succeeded` observations in the current month

## Changes

Adds five per-SDK counters to the usage report: `web`, `ios`, `react_native`, `android`, `flutter` (`*_logs_records_in_period`).

title: chore(ci): speed up async migration tests
body:
## Problem

The async migrations Backend CI job runs a small test set, but it was paying the same full test database setup cost as larger Django shards. That cost showed up as several minutes of pytest setup time before the actual async migration tests ran. The job also previously selected tests by marker across the repository, which collected and deselected tens of thousands of unrelated tests.

Because Backend CI is heavily sharded, this is primarily a runner-minute and feedback-speed improvem

title: fix(warehouse): require db password reentry on ssh tunnel change
body:
## Problem

An editor with update permissions on an external data source could exfiltrate the stored database password by swapping in a malicious SSH tunnel. The DB password is redacted in API responses but kept server-side and merged into the new configuration on update. By pointing the tunnel at an attacker-controlled host (or enabling a tunnel that wasn't enabled before), the editor could make the backend authenticate to a server they control and capture the credential off the wire.

## Chang

title: feat(hogli): add devbox region support

`hogli devbox` had no concept of region — every box silently landed in `us-east-1`, with no way to launch in the new `eu-central-1` (Frankfurt) region, and the list/status views didn't show which region a box was in.

## Changes

- `devbox:start` gains `--region` (`us-east-1` | `eu-central-1`, default `us-east-1`), forwarded to the template's `workspace_region` parameter.
- `devbox:list` gains a `REGION` column; `devbox:status` gains a `Region:` line, both read from the worksp

## Problem

The new hog-charts lifecycle chart (behind `PRODUCT_ANALYTICS_HOG_CHARTS_LIFECYCLE`) sorts series in the opposite order of the legacy lifecycle chart. The legacy chart in `trendsDataLogic.ts:197` orders results descending (dormant → returning → resurrecting → new), so dormant appears at the top of the legend and the positive bars stack returning → resurrecting → new bottom-up. The new chart was sorting ascending, putting new at the top of the legend and reversing the positive stack.

title: feat(property-vals): add two-stage aggregation to collapse pod amplification
body:
## Problem

property-vals-rs aggregates `(team, type, key, value)` tuples in each pod's local HashMap and flushes to `clickhouse_property_values` every 30s. The input topic `team_event_partitioned_events_json` keys by `team_id-event-random(0..6)`, which spreads a single team's events across most of the 128 partitions. With N pods, the same low-cardinality tuple like `(team=2, $browser, Chrome)` lives in N different pods' HashMaps, so each pod emits a record for it at flush. ClickHouse ends up co

title: feat(django): split personhog rpcs into batched calls
body:
## Problem

Django's personhog gRPC call sites send unbounded request payloads. If a caller passes thousands of UUIDs or distinct IDs, the entire list goes in a single RPC.

## Changes

Adds shared helpers that chunks RPCs into batches of 500:

- batches `GetPersonsByUuuids`
- batches `GetPersonsByDistinctIdsInTeam`
- batches `GetDistinctIdsForPersons`

Error Handling --

If any batch RPC failes, the exception propagates out to the healper and we fall back to ORM path

Mendral (our CI-insights backend) holds aggregated cross-run intelligence — recurring flakes, occurrence counts, confidence, proposed/merged fixes — that no single CI run can show. ## Summary

- Fix spurious Security workflow failures on master caused by GitHub Actions concurrency group cancelling queued runs when multiple pushes arrive in quick succession (~24 failures/week).

Mirror of #60167 but for product-routed databases instead of persons. Django's `create_test_db` walks the full migration graph (`state_forwards` for every migration) once per database alias, regardless of what `allow_migrate` returns. ## Problem

The `opentelemetry-proto` Rust crate has several upstream deserialization gaps that cause OTLP/JSON metric payloads to silently drop data rather than returning errors:

1. **[upstream-1253]** Empty `value: {}` AnyValue objects fail to deserialize.
2. **[upstream-3328]** Several `fixed64`/`uint64`/`sfixed64` fields (`count`, `zeroCount`, `asInt`, `bucketCounts`, and timestamp fields) lack the `deserialize_string_to_u64` annotation, so the OTLP/JSON spec-canonical string encoding silen

## Problem

The daily trace-clustering coordinator was emitting `$ai_generation_clusters` and `$ai_trace_clusters` events on behalf of teams that had nothing to cluster, and those empty events kept the same teams re-eligible for clustering the next day. The result was a self-perpetuating loop that inflated the apparent set of AI-active teams by ~50% versus reality, and roughly 70% of cluster events in the last week carried `$ai_total_items_analyzed = 0`.

Three colluding bugs:

1. The `Dai

title: fix(error-tracking): gate mcp v3 query path
body:
## Problem

Error tracking MCP query endpoints should follow the product's V3 query rollout instead of always using the legacy query path. Without the feature-flag gate, MCP responses can diverge from the UI query path when V3 is enabled for a project.

## Changes

- Gate the MCP error tracking list, detail, and issue events endpoints on the `error-tracking-force-query-v3` feature flag.

title: chore(capture): reduce client-error log noise in analytics, AI, and replay paths
body:
## Problem

Capture sits at the network edge: a single misconfigured SDK or scraper can produce thousands of malformed requests per second. Today the v0, payload, AI, and OTel handlers log every one of these at `warn!` or `error!`, even though metrics already classify them. The result is high-volume noise that buries the few logs operators actually want to see — Kafka outages, S3 errors, gzip-bomb attempts.

This PR adopts v1's existing discipline (`log_level()` mapping 4xx → WARN, 5xx → ERROR)

Any saved HogQL that still calls it with a single argument — insights with `math_hogql`, data warehouse model queries, ad-hoc saved queries written before #58714 — started failing with `QueryError: Invalid argument reference in function 'toFloatOrDefault'` (or, once #59835 lands, the standard ari

`tools/hedgebox-dummy/` is the standalone Next.js demo app used to generate realistic session recordings for Hedgebox demo data. It's deliberately excluded from the root pnpm workspace (`!tools/hedgebox-dummy` in `pnpm-workspace.yaml`) with its own `package.json` and lockfile, so **the main CI never installs or builds it** and its dependencies had drifted badly. `pnpm audit` inside the fixture reported **40 advisories (14 high, 24 moderate, 2 low)**, dominated by an old `next@14.2.35

title: chore(deps): bump nodemailer to 8.x to fix SMTP injection
body:
## Problem

Standard caveat about how npm audit is broken, see https://overreacted.io/npm-audit-broken-by-design/

Let's bump nodemailer to a version that audit doesn't complain about

Claude code continues:

`nodemailer@7.x` carries two SMTP command-injection advisories, both fixed only in `nodemailer@8.x`:
- **GHSA-vvjj-xcjg-gr5g** (MODERATE): CRLF injection via the EHLO/HELO transport name (fixed 8.0.5)
- **GHSA-c7w3-x93f-qmm8** (LOW): command injection via unsanitized `envelope.siz

title: chore(deps): bump terser-webpack-plugin to drop serialize-javascript
body:
## Problem

Standard caveat about how `npm audit` is broken, see https://overreacted.io/npm-audit-broken-by-design/

Let's bump `terser-webpack-plugin` so it no longer transitively includes a version of `serialize-javascript` that audit complains about

Claude code continues:

`serialize-javascript@6.x` carries a HIGH-severity RCE (GHSA-5c6j-r48x-rmvq, via `RegExp.flags` / `Date.prototype.toISOString`, CVSS 8.1) and a moderate CPU-exhaustion DoS (GHSA-qj8w-gfj5-8c6v), both fixed in 7.0.3

## Problem

The public interview page (`/interview/<token>`) is rendered via the exporter SPA, which hard-disables the PostHog JS client at module load (`window.JS_POSTHOG_API_KEY = undefined`). That blanket-disable exists to protect *embedded* dashboards from leaking traffic on customers' sites — but the interview page isn't embedded anywhere. It's our own hosted page, opened by an invitee on a link we sent, on our domain.

Without client-side tracking we can only see what server-side webhook e

title: fix(mcp): cap ui-apps build concurrency to avoid oom
body:
## Problem

The MCP container image build OOM-kills Vite (exit 137, e.g. `generated/session-summary`) at the `build:ui-apps` step. `build-ui-apps.ts` defaulted concurrency to `apps.length` and only capped it to 4 when `process.env.CI` was set — but Docker `RUN` layers don't inherit the runner's `CI` env, so inside the image build all 28 apps spawned at once and saturated the builder (~98% peak memory in Depot telemetry).

## Changes

Cap the default to `Math.min(apps.length, 4)` and drop t

body:
> 🪜 Stacked on top of #60153 — review/merge that one first. Diff shown here is just the PR2 delta.

## Problem

Postgres has had per-column sync selection for a while. Every other SQL source we ingest from — MySQL, MSSQL, BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift — was hard-coded to `SELECT *`. That inflates sync cost on wide tables, leaks PII columns into HogQL, and trips up customers who want to exclude expensive blob/JSON columns from their warehouse.

PR1 hoisted the column-projection logic into shared

Column selection (the `ColumnSelectionModal` picker that lets a user pick a subset of source columns to sync into the warehouse) currently works **only for Postgres** — both direct-query and warehouse-managed modes. Every other SQL source (MySQL, MSSQL, BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift) is forced into `SELECT *`, which inflates sync cost, leaks PII columns into HogQL, and degrades sync reliability on wide tables.

This is the **first of two PRs** that close that gap. It's a pure refacto

## Problem

Two failure modes show up a lot in agent-generated diffs (Opus 4.7 especially, but it's broader):

1. Verbose narration of what the code already says.

title: fix(feature-flags): preserve multivariate shape when syncing from API

## Problem

Running `sync-feature-flags` (i.e. `python manage.py sync_feature_flags_from_api`) against the production `/flags?v=2` endpoint always created new local feature flags as **boolean**, even when the production flag was multivariate. That made it impossible to bootstrap a local dev project with multivariate flags — they'd all come back as plain booleans and the existing multivariate workflow would break.

title: fix(build): cut esbuild peak memory to avoid builder oom
body:
## Problem

The Docker `[frontend-build]` step intermittently OOM-kills the Depot builder (surfaces in CI as `keepalive ping failed to receive ACK within timeout`). The killed process is esbuild's Go service: the frontend build runs 8 esbuild builds via an unbounded `Promise.all`, and the two heavy full-app bundles (`PostHog App`, `Exporter`) keep their build graphs resident at once. Contexts are never disposed until the process exits, so every graph piles up — the summed peak hits the builder's

`RetentionBaseQueryBuilder.get_first_time_anchor_expr` is hard-coded to `minIf(events.timestamp, <events_entity_predicate>)`. When a user runs a slash command like `/usage`, the chat agent produces a deterministic `AssistantMessage` from Python code — the handler queries real billing data, formats a report, and persists it as an `AssistantMessage`. The message ends up in conversation history.

title: fix(trends): strip outer user alias from math=hogql expression
body:
## Problem

ClickHouse's new analyzer (`allow_experimental_analyzer=1`) has a bug resolving `ORDER BY <position>` when the SELECT projection contains a nested alias. It rebuilds the projection tree to resolve the position, and the rebuild drops the inner alias — so the analyzer ends up with two AST-different expressions bound to the same outer alias and fires `MULTIPLE_EXPRESSIONS_FOR_ALIAS` (code 179). Confirmed against prod CH 25.12 with a minimal `events`-only repro: `ORDER BY 1` fails, `ORDE

title: feat(llm-gateway): include reset_at in usage response
body:
## Problem

Clients polling the `/v1/usage` endpoint rely on `resets_in_seconds` to know when rate-limit windows reset, but because the underlying Redis window is rolling, this value drifts between polls and is unreliable for scheduling UI updates or displaying accurate countdowns. Additionally, there was no way for clients to know when a user's billing period ends without a separate API call.

## Changes

- Added a `reset_at` field (absolute UTC `datetime`) to `CostLimitStatus`, computed once p

Customers (notably in markets where WhatsApp is the primary comms channel) want to drive merchant drip campaigns and transactional messages from PostHog Workflows, tied to A/B testing cohorts. Today PostHog has Twilio SMS but no WhatsApp connector, which blocks migrating those drip campaigns onto PostHog.

Refs #35049

## Changes

### WhatsApp Cloud API destination

title: feat(api): Allow PAT access to debug_ch_queries slowest_queries

## Problem

`/api/debug_ch_queries/slowest_queries/` only accepts session cookies, so automation (e.g. a Slack agent that posts a daily slow-query digest) can't read it.

## Changes

Add an internal scope `query_performance` and wire it to `slowest_queries` (GET). A staff user's PAT with `query_performance:read` can now call it. `is_staff` still gates. `list` and `precomputation_teams` are unchanged.

`query_performance` is INTERNAL (not exposed in the PAT UI; staff add it to their key via Djang

title: fix(llma): rename skill_identifier back to skill_name on skills endpoints
body:
## Problem

PR #58697 renamed the URL path kwarg on the LLMA skills `get_by_name` / `update_by_name` endpoints from `skill_name` → `skill_identifier`. Because the MCP tool input schemas are code-generated from the Django URL path param names, the public required parameter for `llma-skill-get` and `llma-skill-update` silently changed too.

Every agent and doc still calls these tools with `{"skill_name": "..."}`, so the unrecognized param is dropped, the URL is built with `undefined`, and the call

title: fix(batch-exports): flatten file-download create schema for MCP
body:
## Problem

The `file-download-batch-exports-create` MCP tool fails the `mcp-protocol.test.ts` Hono test suite with a Zod validation error:

```
{
  "code": "invalid_value",
  "values": ["object"],
  "path": ["tools", 40, "inputSchema", "type"],
  "message": "Invalid input: expected \"object\""
}
```

The MCP SDK validates that every tool's `inputSchema.type === "object"`. The create endpoint uses `PolymorphicProxySerializer` to render `oneOf` on the `model` field, which drf-spectacular → Orval

title: fix(devex): make auto-reviewer assignment resilient to stale team slugs
body:
## Problem

GitHub's `POST /pulls/{n}/requested_reviewers` rejects the whole batch with 422 if any team in `team_reviewers` isn't a collaborator on the repo. A single stale team slug in `CODEOWNERS-soft` or a `product.yaml` quietly drops every reviewer (teams **and** users) for affected PRs. So plenty of PRs landed without auto-assigned reviewers and nobody noticed.

Existing `product:lint` had an owners check, but it queried `/orgs/{org}/teams` and silently skipped in CI when the default `GITHU

body:
## Problem

PR #59741 replaces the per-schema `delete_external_data_schedule(...)` loop in `ExternalDataSourceViewSet.destroy` with a single bulk call. The `create` path wraps its bulk call in `try/except`, but the `destroy` path does not — so a failure inside `bulk_delete_external_data_schedules` (most notably an `async_connect()` failure if Temporal is briefly unreachable) propagates out of the handler.

By that point the atomic block above has already committed the soft-deletes for the source

body:
## Problem

The `personhog-replica` service was performing a full JSON parse on every DB read for `group_properties`, `properties`, `properties_last_updated_at`, and `properties_last_operation` — deserializing these JSONB columns into `serde_json::Value` trees, then immediately re-serializing them to bytes when building the gRPC response. Nothing in the service ever inspects these values; they are opaque blobs passed straight through to callers.

title: fix(llma): guard mcp sessions detail loader against stale responses
body:
## Problem

On `/project/:id/mcp-analytics/sessions`, clicking session rows in quick
succession can leave the detail panel displaying tool calls for a previously
clicked row. Row selection updates synchronously via a reducer, but the
`loadToolCalls` loader fires a network request per click with no staleness
guard — when responses arrive out of order, the late-arriving (stale)
response overwrites the freshest one in the store, so the highlighted row
and the tool calls shown for it disagree.

## C

title: feat(experiments): exclude holdouts and excluded variants from the exposure query
body:
## Problem

The exposure query runner was inflating the "exposures" surface with users who didn't actually experience the experiment. Two related issues:

- Holdout users were appearing as a third variant in the exposure timeseries, total exposures, and the SRM chi-square. By definition, a holdout user was never exposed to the experiment, so they don't belong in the "who participated" view. Including them conflated "users assigned to a variant" with "users withheld from the experiment".

The HealthCheck framework is logically owned by Temporal — every active check is registered as a `HealthCheck` subclass under `posthog/temporal/health_checks/` and runs as a Temporal workflow. But four Temporal modules still imported from `posthog.dags.common.health.*`, and two web-analytics checks (`no_live_events`, `no_pageleave_events`) were double-wired as Dagster jobs even though their Temporal equivalents are already registered.

This PR makes the HealthCheck system fully self-

When a user opens a HogFunction from a list page (error-tracking alerts, insight alerts, logs alerts, the upcoming health alerts), the back-arrow on the HogFunction configuration scene returns them to the generic destinations list — not to the page that surfaced the alert in the first place. That's a poor breadcrumb experience for any product that surfaces internal-destination HogFunctions via a dedicated wizard.

## Problem

PostHog has ~10 concrete health checks under `posthog/temporal/health_checks/` (SDK outdated, ingestion warnings, external data failures, web-vitals gaps, no-pageleave, scroll depth, authorized URLs, reverse proxy, materialized view failures, …). The original SDK Doctor alerting PR (#58211) wired Slack/Discord/Teams/email/webhook destinations to **one** of those kinds via a bespoke event name (`$sdk_doctor_alert_firing`), a bespoke `HogFunctionConfigurationContextId`, a bespoke per-t

title: feat(health-checks): return lifecycle deltas from bulk_upsert/bulk_resolve
body:
## Problem

The Temporal health-check orchestrator wants to fire alert events on `HealthIssue` lifecycle transitions — once when a row becomes ACTIVE, once when it transitions ACTIVE → RESOLVED. Today `bulk_upsert` and `bulk_resolve` return only `int` counts, so the per-row delta isn't exposed to the orchestrator.

This PR adds the plumbing. No alerts are emitted yet — that's the next PR in the stack ([#59984](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/59984)).

**Model — `posthog/model

- Add `v1_sink` lifecycle handle to `LifecycleHandles`, registered conditionally when `CAPTURE_V1_SINKS` is non-empty
